items.label: Exercise 3.1 - Not a Topology
items.statement: Let $X = \{a, b, c, d\}$. Show that  $ \mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{b, c, d\}, X\}$ is not a topology on $X$. Find a topology $\mathcal{T}'$ (different from the discrete topology) on $X$ such that $\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}'$.

solution_steps.label: Argument
solution_steps.content: To show that $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology, we need to check if it satisfies the \textit{three axioms of a topology}. $$\begin{aligned} \emptyset \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ and } X \in \mathcal{T} \\ \bigcup_{\alpha} U_\alpha \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ for any collection } \{U_\alpha\} \subseteq \mathcal{T} \\ \bigcap_{i=1}^n U_i \in \mathcal{T} & \text{ for any finite collection } U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n \in \mathcal{T} \end{aligned}$$

solution_steps.label: Violation of T3
solution_steps.content: We can check that $\mathcal{T}$ satisfies T1 and T2, but it fails T3. For example, consider the sets $U_1 = \{a, b\}$ and $U_2 = \{b, c, d\}$, both of which are in $\mathcal{T}$. The intersection $U_1 \cap U_2 = \{b\}$ is not in $\mathcal{T}$, which violates T3. Therefore, $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology on $X$.

solution_steps.label: Refinement to a Topology
solution_steps.content: To find a topology $\mathcal{T}'$ such that $\mathcal{T} \subseteq \mathcal{T}'$, close the displayed family under the missing finite intersections. Since $\{a,b\}\cap\{b,c,d\}=\{b\}$ and $\{a,b,c\}\cap\{b,c,d\}=\{b,c\}$, define $$\mathcal{T}'=\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{b\},\{a,b\},\{b,c\},\{a,b,c\},\{b,c,d\},X\}.$$ This family contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, is closed under arbitrary unions, and is closed under finite intersections, so it is a topology on $X$ containing $\mathcal{T}$.

solution_steps.label: Conclusion
solution_steps.content: Thus $\mathcal{T}$ is not a topology because it fails finite-intersection closure, while the enlarged family $\mathcal{T}'$ above is a non-discrete topology containing $\mathcal{T}$.

items.label: Exercise 3.2
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be non-empty, fix $x_0\in X$, and define $\mathcal{T}=\{U\subseteq X\mid x_0\notin U\text{ or }X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}$. ### Solution $\emptyset\in\mathcal{T}$ because it omits $x_0$, and $X\in\mathcal{T}$ because $X\setminus X=\emptyset$ is finite. For arbitrary unions, let $V=\bigcup U_\lambda$. If $x_0\notin V$, then $V\in\mathcal{T}$. If $x_0\in V$, then $x_0\in U_{\lambda_0}$ for some $\lambda_0$, so $X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite and $X\setminus V\subseteq X\setminus U_{\lambda_0}$ is finite. For finite intersections $W=\bigcap_{i=1}^nU_i$, if some $U_i$ omits $x_0$, so does $W$; otherwise every $X\setminus U_i$ is finite and $X\setminus W=\bigcup_i(X\setminus U_i)$ is finite. Thus $\mathcal{T}$ is a topology.

items.label: Exercise 3.3
items.statement:
Let X be a set, and let 𝐴 be a subset of X. Define the coarsest topology on X
such that 𝐴 is open in X.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be a set and $A\subseteq X$. Coarser means contained as a collection of open sets. ### Solution The coarsest topology on $X$ in which $A$ is open is $\mathcal{T}_A=\{\emptyset,A,X\}$. This is a topology because all unions and finite intersections of these three sets are again among these three sets. If $\mathcal{T}$ is any topology with $A\in\mathcal{T}$, then $\emptyset,X\in\mathcal{T}$ by the topology axioms, so $\mathcal{T}_A\subseteq\mathcal{T}$. Hence $\mathcal{T}_A$ is the desired coarsest topology.

items.label: Exercise 3.4
items.statement:
Show that the discrete topology disc is finer than the cofinite topology cof on
any set X.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$ and $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}=\{U\subseteq X\mid X\setminus U\text{ is finite}\}\cup\{\emptyset\}$. ### Solution Every $U\in\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}$ is a subset of $X$, and every subset of $X$ lies in $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Therefore $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{cof}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$, so the discrete topology is finer. If $X$ is infinite this containment is strict because singletons are discrete-open but not cofinite-open; if $X$ is finite the two topologies agree.

items.label: Exercise 3.5
items.statement:
Let X = {a, b, c, d}. Find two topologies 1 and 2 with 1 ≠ 2 such that a
bijection 𝑓 ∶ X → X is a homeomorphism (where the domain is given 1 as topology and the
codomain is given 2 as topology).
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X=\{a,b,c,d\}$. A homeomorphism is a bijection whose map and inverse are continuous. ### Solution Let $f$ swap $a$ and $b$ and fix $c,d$. Set $\mathcal{T}_1=\{\emptyset,\{a\},\{a,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$ and $\mathcal{T}_2=\{f(U)\mid U\in\mathcal{T}_1\}=\{\emptyset,\{b\},\{b,c\},\{a,b,c\},X\}$. Both are topologies by direct closure under unions and finite intersections. They are distinct since $\{a\}\in\mathcal{T}_1$ but $\{a\}\notin\mathcal{T}_2$. For each open set in $\mathcal{T}_2$, its preimage under $f$ is the corresponding open set in $\mathcal{T}_1$; since $f=f^{-1}$, the inverse is continuous by the same check. Thus the two spaces are homeomorphic with unequal topologies.

items.label: Exercise 3.6
items.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let 𝐴 and 𝐵 be subsets of X.
(a) Assume that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵. Show that 𝐴 ⊆ 𝐵.
(b) Show that 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵 = 𝐴 ∪ 𝐵.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X$ be a topological space and $A,B\subseteq X$. The closure $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$. ### Solution If $A\subseteq B$, every closed set containing $B$ also contains $A$, so $\overline{A}$ is contained in every closed superset of $B$ and hence $\overline{A}\subseteq\overline{B}$. For unions, the first part gives $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}\subseteq\overline{A\cup B}$. Conversely $\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$ is closed as a finite union of closed sets and contains $A\cup B$, so minimality gives $\overline{A\cup B}\subseteq\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$. Therefore $\overline{A\cup B}=\overline{A}\cup\overline{B}$.

items.label: Exercise 3.7
items.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let 𝐴 be a subset of X. Show that 𝑥 ∈ 𝐴 if
and only if every neighborhood of 𝑥 intersects 𝐴.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution:
### Setup and Definitions Let $A\subseteq X$. A neighbourhood of $x$ is an open set containing $x$, and $\overline{A}$ is the smallest closed set containing $A$. ### Solution If some neighbourhood $U$ of $x$ satisfies $U\cap A=\emptyset$, then $A\subseteq X\setminus U$, where $X\setminus U$ is closed. Hence $\overline{A}\subseteq X\setminus U$, so $x
otin\overline{A}$. Conversely, if $x
otin\overline{A}$, then there is a closed set $C\supseteq A$ with $x
otin C$. Then $U=X\setminus C$ is an open neighbourhood of $x$ and $U\cap A=\emptyset$. Thus $x\in\overline{A}$ iff every neighbourhood of $x$ meets $A$.

items.label: Exercise 3.8
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 3.
items.solution: ### Setup and Definitions Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology and $S=\{a,b\}$. A set is dense iff its closure is all of $X$. ### Solution Closed sets are complements of listed open sets. The closed sets containing $S$ are exactly $X$ and $\{a,b,c\}$, so $\overline{S}=X\cap\{a,b,c\}=\{a,b,c\}$. This is not $X$, so $S$ is not dense. Equivalently, the non-empty open set $\{d,e\}$ is disjoint from $S$.

items.label: Exercise 4.1
items.statement:
Let X = {a, b, c, d, 𝑒}, and let
 = {∅, {a, b}, {a, b, d, 𝑒}, {b}, {b, d, 𝑒}, {b, c, d, 𝑒}, {c, d, 𝑒}, {d, 𝑒}, X}
be a topology on X. Show that  = {{a, b}, {b, d, 𝑒}, {c, d, 𝑒}} is a subbasis for  . Is  ′ =
{{a, b}, {b, c, d, 𝑒}, {d, 𝑒}} a subbasis for  ?
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $X=\{a,b,c,d,e\}$ with the listed topology $\mathcal{T}$. ### Solution For $\mathcal{S}=\{\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\}\}$, the union is $X$. Its finite intersections give basis elements $\{a,b\},\{b,d,e\},\{c,d,e\},\{b\},\{d,e\}$, whose unions are exactly the members of $\mathcal{T}$. Thus $\mathcal{S}$ is a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$. For $\mathcal{S}'=\{\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\}\}$, the generated basis is $\{a,b\},\{b,c,d,e\},\{d,e\},\{b\}$. Unions of these sets cannot produce $\{c,d,e\}$, which is in $\mathcal{T}$, so $\mathcal{S}'$ is not a subbasis for $\mathcal{T}$.

items.label: Exercise 4.2
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all open intervals (a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ a < 𝑥 < b} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
standard topology on ℝ denoted by std .
(b) Show that std = d where d is the metric topology obtained from the metric d(𝑥, 𝑦) =
|𝑥 − 𝑦|.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{R},a<b\}$. ### Solution For every $x$, $(x-1,x+1)$ contains $x$, so B1 holds. If $x\in(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, then $x\in(\max(a,c),\min(b,d))\subseteq(a,b)\cap(c,d)$, so B2 holds. Thus $\mathcal{B}$ is a basis. Metric balls for $|x-y|$ are intervals $(x-r,x+r)$, and every interval around a point contains such a ball by choosing $r=\min(x-a,b-x)$. Hence this basis generates the usual metric topology.

items.label: Exercise 4.3
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{S}=\{(a,\infty)\mid a\in\mathbb{R}\}\cup\{(-\infty,b)\mid b\in\mathbb{R}\}$. ### Solution The union of the subbasis elements is $\mathbb{R}$. Finite intersections are rays, empty sets, or intervals $(a,b)=(a,\infty)\cap(-\infty,b)$. Thus every standard basis interval is generated. Conversely, each ray is a union of standard open intervals, e.g. $(a,\infty)=\bigcup_{n=1}^\infty(a,a+n)$. Therefore the topology generated by $\mathcal{S}$ is exactly the standard topology.

items.label: Exercise 4.4
items.statement:
Let ℚ deonte the set of rational numbers, and let ℝ denote the set of real numbers.
Show that
 = {(a, b) ∣ a < b, a, b ∈ ℚ}
is a basis for the standard topology on ℝ where (a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣ a < 𝑥 < b}.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}_{\mathbb{Q}}=\{(a,b)\mid a,b\in\mathbb{Q},a<b\}$. ### Solution Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives rational $a<x<b$ for each real $x$, so B1 holds. Intersections of rational-endpoint intervals are rational-endpoint intervals, so B2 holds. Every such interval is standard-open. Conversely, if $x\in(a,b)$ with real endpoints, choose rationals $p,q$ with $a<p<x<q<b$; then $(p,q)\subseteq(a,b)$. Hence these intervals generate the standard topology.

items.label: Exercise 4.5
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all half-open intervals of the form [a, b) = {𝑥 ∈ ℝ ∣
a ⩽ 𝑥 < b} in ℝ.
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℝ. The topology generated by  is called the
lower limit topology on ℝ.
(b) Find the closure of the subset (0, 1) of ℝ given the lower limit topology.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}_\ell=\{[a,b)\mid a<b\}$ and let $\mathcal{T}_\ell$ be the lower limit topology. ### Solution B1 follows from $x\in[x,x+1)$. B2 follows from $[a,b)\cap[c,d)=[\max(a,c),\min(b,d))$ when the intersection is non-empty. The closure of $(0,1)$ is $[0,1)$: first, $[0,1)$ is closed because its complement $(-\infty,0)\cup[1,\infty)$ is lower-limit-open. Thus the closure is contained in $[0,1)$. Every neighbourhood of $0$ contains some $[0,\varepsilon)$ meeting $(0,1)$, while $[1,2)$ is a neighbourhood of $1$ disjoint from $(0,1)$. Therefore $\overline{(0,1)}=[0,1)$.

items.label: Exercise 4.6
items.statement:
For each 𝑛 ∈ ℤ, let
{
{𝑛} if 𝑛 is odd,
𝐵(𝑛) =
{𝑛 − 1, 𝑛, 𝑛 + 1} if 𝑛 is even.
Show that the collection  = {𝐵(𝑛) ∣ 𝑛 ∈ ℤ} is a basis for a topology on ℤ. The topology gen-
erated by  is known as the digital line topology on ℤ. See [1, pp. 44–46] for some applications
of this topology.
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup For $n\in\mathbb{Z}$, let $B(n)=\{n\}$ if $n$ is odd and $B(n)=\{n-1,n,n+1\}$ if $n$ is even. ### Solution Each $n$ lies in $B(n)$, so B1 holds. For B2, if an intersection contains an odd point $x$, then $B(x)=\{x\}$ refines the intersection. If it contains an even point $x$, then the only basis element with even center $x$ supplies the needed local refinement; neighbouring odd basis elements are singletons and cannot contain $x$. Hence every point in an intersection has a basis neighbourhood contained in that intersection, so the collection is a basis.

items.label: Exercise 4.7
items.statement:
Let  be the collection of all subsets of the form 𝐴a,b = {a𝑧 + b ∣ 𝑧 ∈ ℤ} of ℤ,
where a, b ∈ ℤ and a ≠ 0. (The set 𝐴a,b is known as an arithmetic progression.)
(a) Show that  is a basis for a topology on ℤ.
(b) Show that there are infinitely many primes by using the topology generated by . (This
topology is known as the arithmetic progression topology on ℤ and it was used originally
by Furstenberg [3] to show that there are infinitely many primes.)
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup For $a\ne0$, define $A_{a,b}=\{az+b\mid z\in\mathbb{Z}\}$. ### Solution B1 holds because $A_{1,0}=\mathbb{Z}$. If $n\in A_{a,b}\cap A_{c,d}$, then the intersection is the congruence class $A_{\operatorname{lcm}(|a|,|c|),n}$, so B2 holds. Each basis element is clopen because its complement is a finite union of the other residue classes modulo $|a|$. Every non-empty open set is infinite. If there were finitely many primes $p_i$, then $\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{-1,1\}=\bigcup_iA_{p_i,0}$ would be closed, so $\{-1,1\}$ would be open, contradicting that no non-empty finite set is open. Therefore there are infinitely many primes.

items.label: Exercise 4.8
items.statement:
Let X be a topological space, and let  be a basis for the topology on X. Show
that a subset 𝐴 of X is dense in X if and only if every non-empty basis element in  intersects
𝐴. (Recall that a set 𝑈 intersects a set 𝑉 if 𝑈 ∩ 𝑉 ≠ ∅.)
items.intuition: Practice problem from Chapter 4.
items.solution: ### Setup Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for $X$ and $A\subseteq X$. ### Solution If $A$ is dense, every non-empty open set meets $A$, hence every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Conversely, assume every non-empty basis element meets $A$. Given a non-empty open set $U$, choose $x\in U$ and a basis element $B$ with $x\in B\subseteq U$. Then $B\cap A\ne\emptyset$, so $U\cap A\ne\emptyset$. Thus every non-empty open set meets $A$, and $A$ is dense.

items.statement: A \textbf{based map} $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ is a continuous map $h\colon X\to Y$ with $h(x_0)=y_0$.
items.intuition: A continuous map that respects chosen basepoints. Based maps are what induce homomorphisms of fundamental groups.
items.label: Based Map
items.gloss: A continuous map that respects chosen basepoints. Based maps are what induce homomorphisms of fundamental groups.
items.example: Composing a loop at $x_0$ with $h$ gives a loop at $y_0$.

items.statement: If $X$ is path-connected and $x_0,x_1\in X$, then $\pi_1(X,x_0)\cong\pi_1(X,x_1)$.
items.intuition: In a path-connected space the fundamental group does not depend (up to isomorphism) on the chosen basepoint — so one often writes $\pi_1(X)$.
items.label: Basepoint Independence
items.gloss: In a path-connected space the fundamental group does not depend (up to isomorphism) on the chosen basepoint — so one often writes $\pi_1(X)$.
items.example: A path $\alpha$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ induces the isomorphism $\hat\alpha([f])=[\alpha]^{-1}*[f]*[\alpha]$.
items.proof: Let $\alpha: I \to X$ be a path from $x_0$ to $x_1$. If $f: I \to X$ is a loop in $X$ based $x_0$, then $\alpha * f * \alpha: \hat{\pi}_1 (X, x_0 ) \to \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$ by is a loop in $X$ based at $x_1$. We define the map $\alpha ([\hat{f} ]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha] \alpha$ where we have used the fact that [ $\alpha ] = [\alpha]^{-1}$. It is a well-defined map as $*$ is well-defined, and moreover, it only depends on the path homotopy class of $\alpha$. We will show that $\alpha: \hat{\pi}_1 (X, x_0 ) \to \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$ is an isomorphism. We will first show that $\alpha$ is a homomorphism, and then show that it is bijective. Since $([\hat{f} ] * [g]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [g] * [\alpha] \alpha = ([\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha]) * ([\alpha]^{-1} * [g] * [\alpha]) =\alpha ([\hat{f} ]) * \alpha ([\hat{g}])$, for all $[f ], [g] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$, it follows that $\alpha$ is a homomorphism. To show that $\alpha$ is bijective, and hence, an isomorphism, we show that $\alpha$ has an inverse. Let $\beta = \alpha$, i.e., $\beta$ is the reverse path of $\alpha$. Since $\hat{\beta}([h]) = [\beta]^{-1} * [h] * [\beta] = [\alpha] * [h] * [\alpha]^{-1} \beta([h])) = [\alpha]^{-1} * ([\beta]^{-1} * [h] * [\beta]) * [\alpha] = [h] ( \hat{\alpha}$ for all $[h] \in \pi_1 (X, x_1 )$, and $([\hat{f} ]) = [\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha] = [\beta] * [f ] * [\beta]^{-1} \alpha \hat{\alpha} ([f ])) = [\beta]^{-1} * ([\alpha]^{-1} * [f ] * [\alpha]) * [\beta] = [f ] \beta( \hat{\alpha} )^{-1}$. for all $[f ] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$, it follows that $\hat{\beta} = ($

items.statement: A \textbf{binary operation} on a set $S$ is a map $*\colon S\times S\to S$; one writes $a*b$ for $*(a,b)$.
items.intuition: A rule combining two elements of $S$ into a third, with $S$ closed under it. The raw ingredient of a group.
items.label: Binary Operation
items.gloss: A rule combining two elements of $S$ into a third, with $S$ closed under it. The raw ingredient of a group.
items.example: Addition and multiplication on $\mathbb{Z}$ are binary operations.

items.statement: In a metric space $(X,d)$, a subset $A$ is \textbf{bounded} if there is $M\in\mathbb{R}$ with $d(a_1,a_2)\le M$ for all $a_1,a_2\in A$.
items.intuition: The set fits inside some ball of finite radius — its points are never more than $M$ apart.
items.label: Bounded Subset
items.gloss: The set fits inside some ball of finite radius — its points are never more than $M$ apart.
items.example: In $\mathbb{R}^n$, $A$ is bounded iff it sits inside some ball about the origin. Boundedness is metric, not topological.

items.statement: $A\times B=\{(a,b)\mid a\in A,\ b\in B\}$, the set of ordered pairs with first coordinate in $A$ and second in $B$.
items.formal_statement: $A\times B=\{(a,b):a\in A\land b\in B\}$.
items.definition: A construction combining two sets into the set of all ordered pairs.
items.intuition: The set underlying product spaces and the ambient set for binary relations.
items.label: Cartesian Product
items.gloss: All pairings of an element of $A$ with an element of $B$ — the set underlying the product topology.
items.example: $\mathbb{R}^2=\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}$; the torus is $S^1\times S^1$.

items.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{cofinite topology} consists of $\varnothing$ together with all $U\subseteq X$ whose complement $X\setminus U$ is finite.
items.intuition: A subset is open exactly when it misses only finitely many points. A natural example of a topology that need not come from a metric.
items.label: Cofinite Topology
items.gloss: A subset is open exactly when it misses only finitely many points. A natural example of a topology that need not come from a metric.
items.example: If $X$ is finite, the cofinite topology equals the discrete topology; if $X$ is infinite it is strictly coarser.

items.statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{compact} if it is compact in the subspace topology; equivalently, every cover of $A$ by open subsets of $X$ has a finite subcollection covering $A$ (Lemma 6.34).
items.formal_statement: $$A\subseteq X\text{ is compact}\Longleftrightarrow (A,\tau_A)\text{ is compact},\quad \tau_A=\{A\cap U:U\in\tau_X\}.$$
items.definition: A subset A of a topological space X is compact if A is compact with the subspace topology. Equivalently, every cover of A by open subsets of X has a finite subcover of A.
items.intuition: Compactness of a subset can be tested with open sets of the ambient space — often more convenient.
items.label: Compact Subspace
items.gloss: Compactness of a subset can be tested with open sets of the ambient space — often more convenient.
items.example: $S^n\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ is compact; the torus $T^2$ is compact.
convention_notes: A subset is compact as a subspace when every open cover in the subspace topology has a finite subcover.
In Hausdorff spaces compact subsets are closed, but compact does not mean closed in arbitrary spaces.

convention_review.selected_meaning: A subset A of X is compact iff A is compact with the subspace topology.
alternative_conventions: In metric/Euclidean contexts students often confuse compact subset with closed and bounded subset.
Some statements use ambient-open covers of A instead of subspace-open covers; these are equivalent by the subspace topology definition.
convention_review.modeling_decision: Keep compact_subspace as definition/concept distinct from compact_space; link it to subspace_topology and compact_space.
convention_review.exam_warning: Compact subset need not be closed unless the ambient space is Hausdorff.

items.statement: For topologies $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ on $X$: if $\mathcal{T}_1\subseteq\mathcal{T}_2$ we say $\mathcal{T}_1$ is \textbf{coarser} and $\mathcal{T}_2$ is \textbf{finer}. They are \textbf{comparable} if one contains the other.
items.intuition: Topologies on a fixed set are partially ordered by inclusion. “Finer” means more open sets (more resolving power); “coarser” means fewer.
items.label: Comparable Topologies (Coarser / Finer)
items.gloss: Topologies on a fixed set are partially ordered by inclusion. “Finer” means more open sets (more resolving power); “coarser” means fewer.
items.example: $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ for every $\mathcal{T}$. Not every pair of topologies is comparable.

items.statement: For $B\subseteq X$, the complement of $B$ in $X$ is $X\setminus B$.
items.formal_statement: $B^c=X\setminus B=\{x\in X\mid x\notin B\}$.
items.definition: The part of the ambient set outside a given subset.
items.intuition: The operation linking open and closed sets.
items.label: Complement
items.gloss: The difference of $A$ and $B$ when an ambient set $A$ is understood. Openness and closedness in topology are dual via complements.
items.example: In a topological space, $K$ is closed iff $K^c$ is open.

items.statement: Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be topological spaces. Then homeomorphism satisfies $$\begin{aligned} \textbf{Reflexivity:} \quad &X \cong X, \\ \textbf{Symmetry:} \quad &X \cong Y \Longrightarrow Y \cong X, \\ \textbf{Transitivity:} \quad &X \cong Y \text{ and } Y \cong Z \Longrightarrow X \cong Z. \end{aligned}$$ Equivalently, the relation $\cong$ of being homeomorphic is an equivalence relation on topological spaces.
items.intuition: Homeomorphism is topology's notion of sameness of shape. Reflexivity says every space has the same shape as itself. Symmetry says a homeomorphism can be reversed. Transitivity says two reversible deformations can be chained.
items.label: Homeomorphism Is an Equivalence Relation
items.gloss: Homeomorphism is topology's notion of sameness of shape. Reflexivity says every space has the same shape as itself. Symmetry says a homeomorphism can be reversed. Transitivity says two reversible deformations can be chained.
items.example: The identity map $\operatorname{id}_X \colon X \to X$ is a homeomorphism, so $X \cong X$. If $f \colon X \to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $f^{-1} \colon Y \to X$ is also a homeomorphism. If $f \colon X \to Y$ and $g \colon Y \to Z$ are homeomorphisms, then $$\begin{aligned} g \circ f \colon X \to Z \end{aligned}$$ is a homeomorphism with inverse $$\begin{aligned} (g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}. \end{aligned}$$
items.proof: The identity map id $: X \to X$ (where the domain and the codomain are equipped with the same topology) is clearly continuous and bijective. As the identity map is its own inverse, then it is also a homeomorphism. Hence, $X \cong X$ and so $\cong$ satisfies the reflexivity condition for an equivalence relation. If $f: X \to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $f^{-1}: Y \to X$ is also a homeomorphism: $f^{-1}$ is a continuous bijection with continuous inverse $(f^{-1} )^{-1} = f: X \to Y$. Hence, $X \cong Y$ if and only if $Y \cong X$. Thus $\cong$ satisfies the symmetry condition for an equivalence relation.

proof_steps.label: Reflexivity
proof_steps.content: The identity map $\operatorname{id}_X:X\to X$ is continuous, bijective, and its own inverse. Hence $\operatorname{id}_X$ is a homeomorphism and $X\cong X$.

proof_steps.label: Symmetry
proof_steps.content: If $f:X\to Y$ is a homeomorphism, then $f^{-1}:Y\to X$ is continuous and bijective with inverse $f$. Hence $X\cong Y$ implies $Y\cong X$.

items.statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ and $g\colon Y\to Z$ are continuous, then $g\circ f\colon X\to Z$ is continuous.
items.intuition: Continuity is preserved under composition — the property that makes topological spaces and continuous maps into a category.
items.label: Composition of Continuous Maps
items.gloss: Continuity is preserved under composition — the property that makes topological spaces and continuous maps into a category.
items.example: Used repeatedly, e.g. to show that being homeomorphic is transitive (Thm. 3.23).
items.proof: Let $W \subseteq Z$ be open in $Z$. We need to prove that $(g\circ f )^{-1} (W )$ is open in $X$. Since $(g\circ f )^{-1} (W ) = \{x \in X \mid g(f (x)) \in W \} = \{x \in X \mid f (x) \in g^{-1} (W )\} = \{x \in X \mid x \in f^{-1} (g^{-1} (W ))\} = f^{-1} (g^{-1} (W ))$ and that $g^{-1} (W )$ is open in $Y$ and $f^{-1} (g^{-1} (W ))$ is open in $X$ (by continuity of $g$ and $f )$, it follows that $(g\circ f )^{-1} (W )$ is open in $X$. Hence, $g\circ f$ is continuous. We can express continuity at a point for maps between topological spaces using neighborhoods. (See Theorem 2.12 for the case of metric spaces.)

proof_steps.label: Pull Back an Open Set
proof_steps.content: Let $W\subseteq Z$ be open. To prove $g\circ f$ is continuous, it is enough to show that $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)$ is open in $X$.

proof_steps.label: Compute the Preimage
proof_steps.content: The preimage satisfies $(g\circ f)^{-1}(W)=f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$.

proof_steps.label: Apply Continuity Twice
proof_steps.content: Since $g$ is continuous, $g^{-1}(W)$ is open in $Y$; since $f$ is continuous, $f^{-1}(g^{-1}(W))$ is open in $X$. Hence $g\circ f$ is continuous.

items.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $g:Y\to Z$, the composition $g\circ f:X\to Z$ is defined by $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$.
items.formal_statement: $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$ whenever $\operatorname{cod}(f)=\operatorname{dom}(g)$.
items.definition: The operation of applying one function after another.
items.intuition: Do $f$ first, then $g$. This is the backbone of continuity and functoriality.
items.label: Composition of Maps
items.gloss: Apply $f$, then $g$. Composition is associative and the identity acts as a unit — the backbone of categorical thinking in topology.
items.example: $\mathrm{id}_B\circ f=f=f\circ\mathrm{id}_A$; continuity and homomorphism properties are preserved under composition.

items.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous at $p$ iff for every neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$ there is a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ with $f(A)\subseteq B$.
items.intuition: Reformulates $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity purely with neighborhoods — the bridge that lets continuity make sense without a metric.
items.label: Continuity at a Point (Nbhd Form)
items.gloss: Reformulates $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity purely with neighborhoods — the bridge that lets continuity make sense without a metric.
items.example: Recovers the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition by taking $B=\mathsf{B}_Y(f(p);\epsilon)$ and $A=\mathsf{B}_X(p;\delta)$.
items.proof: Assume that $f$ is continuous at $p$. If $B$ is a neighborhood of $f (p)$, then, by definition, there is an open ball $\mathsf{B}_Y (f (p); \varepsilon)$ about $f (p)$ that is contained in $B$. Since $f$ is continuous at $p$, there is a $\delta > 0$ such that if $d_X (p, q) < \delta$, then $d_Y (f (p), f (q)) < \varepsilon$. Hence, $f (\mathsf{B}_X (p; \delta)) \subseteq \mathsf{B}_Y (f (p); \varepsilon) \subseteq B$. That is, if we let $A = \mathsf{B}_X (p, \delta)$, then for all neighborhoods $B$ of $f (p)$, we have that $f (A) \subseteq B$ where $A$ is a neighborhood of $p$. Assume that for all neighborhoods $B$ of $f (p)$, there is a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ such that $f (A) \subseteq B$. We need to prove that for all $\varepsilon > 0$, there is a $\delta > 0$ such that if $d_X (p, q) < \delta$, then $d_Y (f (p), f (q)) < \varepsilon$. By utilizing the fact that $B = \mathsf{B}_Y (f (p); \varepsilon)$ is a neighborhood of $f (p)$, then, by assumption, there must be a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ such that $f (A) \subseteq B$. Since $A$ is a neighborhood of $p$, there is an open ball $\mathsf{B}_X (p; \delta)$ about $p$ that is contained in $A$. Now assume that $d_X (p, p' ) < \delta$. Then $p' \in \mathsf{B}_X (p; \delta) \subseteq A$. Thus $f (p' ) \in B = \mathsf{B}_Y (f (p); \varepsilon)$, and hence, $d_Y (f (p), f (p' )) < \varepsilon$. Thus $f$ is continuous at $p$.

proof_steps.label: From Epsilon-Delta to Neighborhoods
proof_steps.content: Assume $f$ is continuous at $p$. Given a neighborhood $B$ of $f(p)$, choose an open ball around $f(p)$ inside $B$, then use the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ condition to find a neighborhood of $p$ mapped into $B$.

proof_steps.label: From Neighborhoods to Epsilon-Delta
proof_steps.content: Conversely, take $B=\mathsf{B}_Y(f(p);\varepsilon)$. The neighborhood condition gives a neighborhood $A$ of $p$ mapped into $B$, and an open ball around $p$ inside $A$ supplies $\delta$.

proof_steps.label: Conclusion
proof_steps.content: The neighborhood formulation and the metric $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ formulation of continuity at $p$ are equivalent.

items.statement: Let $X$ and $Y$ be topological spaces, let $x\in X$, and let $f\colon X\to Y$. The map $f$ is \textbf{continuous at} $x$ if for every neighborhood $V$ of $f(x)$ there is a neighborhood $U$ of $x$ such that $f(U)\subseteq V$. $$\begin{aligned} f\text{ is continuous at }x \quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad \forall V\in\mathcal{N}_Y(f(x))\;\exists U\in\mathcal{N}_X(x)\text{ such that } f(U)\subseteq V. \end{aligned}$$
items.intuition: Local continuity stated with neighborhoods. A map is continuous iff it is continuous at every point (Thm. 3.18).
items.label: Continuity at a Point (Topological)
items.gloss: Local continuity stated with neighborhoods. A map is continuous iff it is continuous at every point (Thm. 3.18).
items.example: Directly generalises the metric neighborhood criterion of Thm. 2.12.

items.statement: If $\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $\mathcal{S}$) is a basis (resp. subbasis) for $Y$, then $f\colon X\to Y$ is continuous iff $f^{-1}(B)$ is open for every $B\in\mathcal{B}$ (resp. $f^{-1}(S)$ open for every $S\in\mathcal{S}$).
items.intuition: To check continuity you need only test preimages of basis (or subbasis) elements, not all open sets — a big practical economy.
items.label: Continuity via a (Sub)basis
items.gloss: To check continuity you need only test preimages of basis (or subbasis) elements, not all open sets — a big practical economy.
items.example: Crucial for proving that projections out of a product are continuous.
items.proof: We prove the statement about basis. Assume that $f$ is continuous. Since each basis element $B \in \mathcal{B}$ is open in $Y$, then by continuity $f^{-1} (B)$ is open in $X$. Assume that for each $B \in \mathcal{B}$ the preimage $f^{-1} (B)$ is open in $X$. Let $\mathcal{T}_Y$ be the topology on $Y. \bigcup$ Since every $V \in \mathcal{T}_Y$ is a union $V = \lambda\in\Lambda B_\lambda$ of basis elements $B_\lambda \in \mathcal{B}$, we have $\bigcup f^{-1} (V ) = f^{-1} (B_\lambda ). \lambda\in\Lambda$ Thus if each $f^{-1} (B_\lambda )$ is open in $X$, so is $f^{-1} (V )$.

proof_steps.label: Continuous Maps Pull Back Basis Elements
proof_steps.content: If $f$ is continuous and $B\in\mathcal{B}$ is a basis element of $Y$, then $B$ is open and $f^{-1}(B)$ is open in $X$.

proof_steps.label: Basis Elements Generate All Open Sets
proof_steps.content: Conversely, every open $V\subseteq Y$ is a union of basis elements. Since preimages commute with unions, $f^{-1}(V)$ is a union of open sets when every basis-element preimage is open.

proof_steps.label: Subbasis Variant
proof_steps.content: The same reduction applies to subbases by first passing from subbasis elements to finite intersections and then to arbitrary unions.

items.statement: Let $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ be metric spaces, and let $f\colon X\to Y$ be a function. Then $f$ is continuous if and only if, for every open set $V\subseteq Y$, the preimage $f^{-1}(V)=\{x\in X: f(x)\in V\}$ is open in $X$. $$ f\colon X\to Y \text{ is continuous} \iff \forall V\subseteq Y,\ \bigl(V \text{ is open in } Y \Rightarrow f^{-1}(V) \text{ is open in } X\bigr). $$
items.intuition: Continuity can be tested entirely by pulling back open sets: open sets in the codomain must have open preimages in the domain. This reformulates the usual distance-based idea of continuity in a way that no longer explicitly mentions distances, which is why it becomes the standard definition in topology.
items.label: Continuity via Preimages of Open Sets
items.gloss: Continuity can be tested entirely by pulling back open sets: open sets in the codomain must have open preimages in the domain. This reformulates the usual distance-based idea of continuity in a way that no longer explicitly mentions distances, which is why it becomes the standard definition in topology.
items.example: This open-set criterion becomes the definition of continuity between topological spaces.
items.proof: Assume that $f$ is continuous. For $B \subseteq Y$ open in $Y$, we have to prove that $f^{-1} (B) \subseteq X$ is open in $X$. Let $a \in f^{-1} (B)$. We want to prove that there is an open ball about $a$ in $X$ that is contained in $f^{-1} (B)$. By assumption, $B \subseteq Y$ is open in $Y$. Hence, there is an $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $\mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon) \subseteq B$. From the assumption that $f$ is continuous there is a $\delta > 0$ such that $\mathsf{B}_X (a; \delta) \subseteq f^{-1} (\mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon)) \subseteq f^{-1} (B)$. We now prove the opposite implication. Assume that for every subset $B \subseteq Y$ open in $Y$, the preimage $f^{-1} (B)$ of $B$ under $f$ is open in $X$. Let $a \in X$ and $\varepsilon > 0$ be a real number. From the first assumption it follows that $f^{-1} (\mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon)) \subseteq X$ is open in $X$. As $f^{-1} (\mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon))$ is open and contains $a$, there is a $\delta > 0$ such that $\mathsf{B}_X (a; \delta) \subseteq f^{-1} (\mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon))$. Thus $x \in \mathsf{B}_X (a; \delta)$ implies that $f (x) \in \mathsf{B}_Y (f (a); \varepsilon)$. Hence, $f: X \to Y$ is continuous. Let $A$ and $B$ be sets, and let $f: A \to B$. Then $f^{-1} (B)$ will always exist even if there is no inverse map. In the cases where $f$ has an inverse there is no ambiguity. If $U$ and $V$ are both subsets of $B$ then $f^{-1} (U \cup V ) = f^{-1} (U ) \cup f^{-1} (V )$ and $f^{-1} (U \cap V ) = f^{-1} (U ) \cap f^{-1} (V )$, and furthermore, if $U \subseteq V$ then $f^{-1} (U ) \subseteq f^{-1} (V )$. Let $U \subseteq A$ and $V \subseteq B$, then ( ) $U \subseteq f^{-1} (f (U ))$ and $f f^{-1} (V ) \subseteq V$. We also note that if $U$ is a subset of $B$ then $f^{-1} (B \setminus U ) = f^{-1} (U^{c} ) = (f^{-1} (U ))c = A \setminus f^{-1} (U )$.

proof_steps.label: Continuous Implies Open Preimages
proof_steps.content: Assume $f$ is continuous and $B\subseteq Y$ is open. For $a\in f^{-1}(B)$, choose an open ball around $f(a)$ inside $B$ and then use continuity to find a ball around $a$ mapped into it.

proof_steps.label: Open Preimages Imply Continuity
proof_steps.content: Conversely, if every open $B\subseteq Y$ has open preimage, apply this to $\mathsf{B}_Y(f(a);\varepsilon)$ and use openness of its preimage to get a suitable $\delta$.

proof_steps.label: Record Set-Theoretic Rules
proof_steps.content: The proof also uses basic preimage rules: preimages commute with unions, intersections, complements, and preserve inclusions. These rules do not require an inverse map.

items.canonical_label: Continuity via preimages of open sets
items.display_label: Continuity via Preimages of Open Sets

items.statement: If $X$ is compact and $f:X\to Y$ is continuous, then $f(X)$ is compact; if $f$ is also surjective, then $Y$ is compact.
items.formal_statement: If $f:X\to Y$ is continuous and $X$ is compact, then $f(X)$ is compact. In particular, if $f$ is surjective, then $Y$ is compact.
items.intuition: Continuity preserves compactness, just as it preserves connectedness. A key step toward the extreme value theorem.
items.label: Continuous Image of a Compact Space
items.gloss: Continuity preserves compactness, just as it preserves connectedness. A key step toward the extreme value theorem.
items.example: Pull an open cover of $Y$ back through $f$, take a finite subcover upstairs, push forward.
items.proof: Let $\{U_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ be an open cover of $f(X)$. The preimages $f^{-1}(U_\lambda)$ form an open cover of $X$ by continuity. Compactness gives finitely many whose preimages cover $X$, and the corresponding $U_\lambda$ cover $f(X)$.

proof_steps.label: Pull Back the Cover
proof_steps.content: Let $\mathcal{C}=\{U_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ be an open cover of $Y$. The family $\{f^{-1}(U_\lambda)\}_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ is an open cover of $X$ by continuity.

proof_steps.label: Use Compactness of the Domain
proof_steps.content: Since $X$ is compact, finitely many pulled-back sets $f^{-1}(U_{\lambda_1}),\ldots,f^{-1}(U_{\lambda_n})$ cover $X$.

proof_steps.label: Push the Subcover Forward
proof_steps.content: The corresponding sets $U_{\lambda_1},\ldots,U_{\lambda_n}$ cover $Y$, so the original cover has a finite subcover. Thus $Y$ is compact.

items.canonical_label: Continuous image of a compact space
items.display_label: Continuous Image of a Compact Space

items.statement: If $X$ is connected, $Y$ a space, and $f\colon X\to Y$ a surjective continuous map, then $Y$ is connected. Hence $f(X)$ is connected for any continuous $f$.
items.intuition: Continuity cannot tear a connected space apart: its image stays in one piece. This makes connectedness a topological invariant.
items.label: Continuous Image of a Connected Space
items.gloss: Continuity cannot tear a connected space apart: its image stays in one piece. This makes connectedness a topological invariant.
items.example: Yields the Intermediate Value Theorem as a special case (Thm. 6.13).
items.proof: We prove the equivalent statement that if $f: X \to Y$ is a surjective continuous map and $Y$ is disconnected then $X$ is disconnected. Assume that $Y$ is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of $Y$. Let the pair $U$ and $V$ be a separation of $Y$. Then $f^{-1} (U )$ and $f^{-1} (V )$ are non-empty subsets of $X$ which are open in $X$ as $f$ is a surjective continuous map. Furthermore, $f^{-1} (U ) \cap f^{-1} (V ) = f^{-1} (U \cap V ) = \varnothing$ and $f^{-1} (U ) \cup f^{-1} (V ) = f^{-1} (U \cup V ) = X$. Hence, the pair $f^{-1} (U )$ and $f^{-1} (V )$ is a separation of $X$. Thus $X$ is disconnected.

proof_steps.label: Use the Contrapositive
proof_steps.content: Prove the equivalent statement: if $f:X\to Y$ is surjective and continuous and $Y$ is disconnected, then $X$ is disconnected.

proof_steps.label: Pull Back a Separation
proof_steps.content: Let $U,V$ be a separation of $Y$. Then $f^{-1}(U)$ and $f^{-1}(V)$ are nonempty open subsets of $X$ because $f$ is surjective and continuous.

proof_steps.label: Separate the Domain
proof_steps.content: The preimages are disjoint and their union is $X$, so they form a separation of $X$. Hence connectedness of $X$ forces connectedness of $Y$.

items.canonical_label: Continuous image of a connected space
items.display_label: Continuous Image of a Connected Space

items.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ between metric spaces is \textbf{continuous at} $p$ if $\forall\,\epsilon>0\ \exists\,\delta>0$ such that $d_X(p,q)<\delta \Rightarrow d_Y(f(p),f(q))<\epsilon$. It is \textbf{continuous} if it is continuous at every $p\in X$.
items.intuition: The familiar $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition: points close in the domain are sent to points close in the codomain. Nearness is measured by the two metrics.
items.label: Continuous Map (Metric)
items.gloss: The familiar $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition: points close in the domain are sent to points close in the codomain. Nearness is measured by the two metrics.
items.example: Every map out of a discrete metric space is continuous; the identity $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, but $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is not.

items.statement: A collection $\mathcal{A}$ of subsets is a \textbf{cover} of $X$ if $X=\bigcup_{A\in\mathcal{A}}A$. It is an \textbf{open cover} if each $A$ is open. $\mathcal{A}'\subseteq\mathcal{A}$ is a \textbf{subcover} if it still covers.
items.intuition: A way of overlaying $X$ with (open) patches. The notion of “every open cover has a finite subcover” defines compactness.
items.label: Cover of a Space
items.gloss: A way of overlaying $X$ with (open) patches. The notion of “every open cover has a finite subcover” defines compactness.
items.example: Any basis or subbasis for $X$ is an open cover of $X$.

items.statement: A surjective continuous $p\colon E\to B$ is a \textbf{covering map} if every $b\in B$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $p^{-1}(U)=\bigsqcup_{\lambda}V_\lambda$ a disjoint union of opens, each mapped homeomorphically onto $U$ by $p$. $E$ \textbf{covers} $B$.
items.intuition: $B$ is “evenly covered”: locally $E$ looks like a stack of disjoint copies of a patch of $B$. The route to computing $\pi_1$ via lifting.
items.label: Covering Space
items.gloss: $B$ is “evenly covered”: locally $E$ looks like a stack of disjoint copies of a patch of $B$. The route to computing $\pi_1$ via lifting.
items.example: $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $t\mapsto e^{2\pi i t}$, is the universal cover of the circle.

items.statement: For $x\in G$ (multiplicative), $\langle x\rangle=\{x^n\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a subgroup — the \textbf{cyclic subgroup generated by} $x$, the smallest subgroup containing $x$. $G$ is \textbf{cyclic} if $G=\langle x'\rangle$ for some $x'$.
items.intuition: The powers of a single element form a subgroup. A whole group generated this way is cyclic; all cyclic groups are abelian.
items.label: Cyclic Subgroup
items.gloss: The powers of a single element form a subgroup. A whole group generated this way is cyclic; all cyclic groups are abelian.
items.example: $\mathbb{Z}=\langle 1\rangle=\langle -1\rangle$ is infinite cyclic. $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ is cyclic.
items.proof: Since $H$ is a subgroup of $G$, it follows that $x^{-1} \in H$. Thus $x_{x-1} = x^{-1} x = x_0 = 1 \in H$. Furthermore, $x_n \in H$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ where $x-m = (x^{-1} )m$. Thus $\langle x\rangle$ is a subgroup of $H$.

items.statement: A homotopy $H\colon X\times I\to X$ is a \textbf{deformation retraction} onto $A$ if $H(x,0)=x$, $H(x,1)\in A$ for all $x$, and $H(a,t)=a$ for all $a\in A$, $t\in I$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{deformation retract}.
items.intuition: Continuously shrink $X$ onto $A$ over time, holding $A$ fixed throughout. It exhibits $X$ and $A$ as homotopy equivalent.
items.label: Deformation Retract
items.gloss: Continuously shrink $X$ onto $A$ over time, holding $A$ fixed throughout. It exhibits $X$ and $A$ as homotopy equivalent.
items.example: $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ deformation retracts onto $S^1$; a disc retracts onto its centre.

items.statement: The difference $A\setminus B$ is the set of elements of $A$ that are not elements of $B$.
items.formal_statement: $A\setminus B=\{x\in A\mid x\notin B\}$.
items.definition: The operation of removing one set from another.
items.intuition: What remains of $A$ after excluding $B$. Complements are relative set differences.
items.label: Difference of Sets
items.gloss: What remains of $A$ after removing $B$. Disjoint from $B$, with $(A\setminus B)\cup B=A\cup B$.

items.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{discrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}=\mathcal{P}(X)$, the power set: \textbf{every} subset is open.
items.intuition: The finest possible topology — a space of isolated points with no interaction. Singletons are open here, which uniquely characterises it.
items.label: Discrete Topology
items.gloss: The finest possible topology — a space of isolated points with no interaction. Singletons are open here, which uniquely characterises it.
items.example: For any set $X$, $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$ is the largest topology; every map out of a discrete space is continuous.

items.statement: For a family $(A_i)_{i\in I}$, the disjoint union is $\bigsqcup_{i\in I}A_i=\bigcup_{i\in I}(A_i\times\{i\})$.
items.formal_statement: $\bigsqcup_{i\in I}A_i=\{(a,i)\mid i\in I,\ a\in A_i\}$.
items.definition: A union whose elements are tagged by their index so pieces remain distinct.
items.intuition: Copies of the same point from different pieces do not accidentally merge.
items.label: Disjoint Union
items.gloss: Combine sets while keeping track of which piece each element came from, so copies never merge. Used to describe $p^{-1}(U)$ for covering maps.
items.example: The preimage of an evenly covered set is a disjoint union of homeomorphic sheets (Def. 8.1).

items.statement: For an equivalence relation $\sim$ on $X$, the \textbf{equivalence class} of $x$ is $[x]=\{y\in X\mid x\sim y\}$, and $X/\!\sim\ =\{[x]\mid x\in X\}$.
items.intuition: Classes partition $X$: any two are equal or disjoint, and together they cover $X$. The quotient set $X/\!\sim$ collects them.
items.label: Equivalence Class
items.gloss: Classes partition $X$: any two are equal or disjoint, and together they cover $X$. The quotient set $X/\!\sim$ collects them.
items.example: Equivalence relations correspond (up to bijection) to surjective maps $X\to A$ (Lemma 5.15).

items.statement: For based maps $h_1\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ and $h_2\colon(Y,y_0)\to(Z,z_0)$: $(h_2\circ h_1)_*=(h_2)_*\circ(h_1)_*$, and $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*=\mathrm{id}$.
items.intuition: $\pi_1$ is a functor: it sends spaces to groups and maps to homomorphisms, respecting identities and composition.
items.label: Functoriality of π₁
items.gloss: $\pi_1$ is a functor: it sends spaces to groups and maps to homomorphisms, respecting identities and composition.
items.example: Immediately gives that homeomorphic based spaces have isomorphic $\pi_1$ (Cor. 7.25).
items.proof: Assume that $f: I \to X$ is a loop in $X$ based at $x_0$. Then $(h_2 \circ h_1 )* ([f ]) = [(h_2 \circ h_1 )\circ f ]$ and ( ) $(h_2 )* (h_1 )* ([f ]) = [h_2 \circ(h_1 \circ f )]$. Since composition of continuous maps is associative, i.e., $(h_2 \circ h_1 )\circ f = h_2 \circ(h_1 \circ f )$, these path homotopy classes must be equal. Hence, $(h_2 \circ h_1 )* = (h_2 )* \circ(h_1 )*$. If id$X: X \to X$ is the identity map, then id$X \circ f = f$, and so, (id$X )* ([f ]) =$ [id$X \circ f ] = [f ]$. Hence, (id$X )*$ is the identity automorphism of $\pi_1 (X, x_0 )$.

proof_steps.label: Composition on Loops
proof_steps.content: For a loop $f$ based at $x_0$, $(h_2\circ h_1)_*([f])=[(h_2\circ h_1)\circ f]$.

proof_steps.label: Use Associativity of Composition
proof_steps.content: Associativity gives $(h_2\circ h_1)\circ f=h_2\circ(h_1\circ f)$, so $(h_2\circ h_1)_*=h_{2*}\circ h_{1*}$.

proof_steps.label: Identity Map Acts as Identity
proof_steps.content: For the identity map, $(\operatorname{id}_X)_*([f])=[\operatorname{id}_X\circ f]=[f]$, so the induced map is the identity automorphism of $\pi_1(X,x_0)$.

items.statement: For a based space $(X,x_0)$, a \textbf{loop} at $x_0$ is a path from $x_0$ to $x_0$. The \textbf{fundamental group} is $\pi_1(X,x_0)=\{[f]\mid f\ \text{a loop at}\ x_0\}$ with operation $*$.
items.intuition: The group of loops at $x_0$ up to path homotopy. The identity is the constant loop and inverses are reversed loops. It measures “holes” the space cannot retract away.
items.label: Fundamental Group
items.gloss: The group of loops at $x_0$ up to path homotopy. The identity is the constant loop and inverses are reversed loops. It measures “holes” the space cannot retract away.
items.example: $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^n,0)=0$ (trivial); $\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\cong\mathbb{Z}$ (Thm. 8.16).

items.statement: For a homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$, the map $\bar\varphi\colon G_1/\ker\varphi\to\operatorname{im}\varphi$, $\bar\varphi(x\ker\varphi)=\varphi(x)$, is an isomorphism, and $\varphi=\bar\varphi\circ\pi$ where $\pi\colon G_1\to G_1/\ker\varphi$.
items.intuition: Any homomorphism factors as a quotient followed by an isomorphism: $G_1/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. The first isomorphism theorem.
items.label: Fundamental Homomorphism Theorem
items.gloss: Any homomorphism factors as a quotient followed by an isomorphism: $G_1/\ker\varphi\cong\operatorname{im}\varphi$. The first isomorphism theorem.
items.example: Identifies the image of $\varphi$ with the quotient of $G_1$ by what $\varphi$ collapses.
items.proof: We first show that $\phi$ is well-defined. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be two elements in $G$. If $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$, we must show that $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. In other words, we must show that $\phi(x_1 ) = \phi(x_2 )$. Since $x_2 \in x_1$ ker $\phi$, there is an element $z$ in ker $\phi$ such that $x_2 = x_1 z$. Thus if $e_2$ is the identity element in $G_2$, we have $\phi(x_2 ) = \phi(x_1 z) = \phi(x_1 )\phi(z) = \phi(x_1 )e_2 = \phi(x_1 )$ where we have used the fact that $\phi$ is a homomorphism. Hence, $\phi$ is a well-defined map. We now show that $\phi$ is an isomorphism, where we first show that it is a homomorphism, and then that it is bijective. By definition for $x_1$ ker $\phi, x_2$ ker $\phi \in G/$ ker $\phi$, we have $\phi((x_1$ ker $\phi)(x_2$ ker $\phi)) = \phi((x_1 x_2 )$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_1 x_2 ) = \phi(x_1 )\phi(x_2 ) = \phi(x_1$ ker $\phi)\phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. Thus $\phi$ is a homomorphism. Now assume that $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. We must show that this implies that $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$. If $\phi(x_1$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x_2$ ker $\phi)$, then by definition, we have $\phi(x_1 ) = \phi(x_2 )$. This implies that $\phi(x_2 )^{-1} \phi(x_1 ) = e_2$. Since $\phi$ is a homomorphism, we have $\phi(x^{-1} 2 x_1 ) = e_2$. Thus $x^{-1} x_1 \in$ ker $\phi$, and $-1$ so, $x_2 x_1$ ker $\phi =$ ker $\phi$. Hence, $x_1$ ker $\phi = x_2$ ker $\phi$. Thus $\phi$ is injective. For any $\phi(x) \in$ im $\phi$, we have, by definition, that $\phi(x$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x)$. Hence, $\phi$ is surjective. Thus $\phi$ is bijective, and hence, it is an isomorphism. By definition, we have, for elements $x_1$ and $x_2$ in $G_1$, that $\pi(x_1 x_2 ) = (x_1 x_2 )$ ker $\phi = (x_1$ ker $\phi)(x_2$ ker $\phi)$. Appendix B. Elementary algebra 109 Hence, $\pi$ is a homomorphism. Furthermore, $(\phi\circ\pi)(x) = \phi(\pi(x)) = \phi(x$ ker $\phi) = \phi(x)$ for each $x \in G_1$. This completes the proof.

items.statement: Every polynomial $z^n+a_{n-1}z^{n-1}+\cdots+a_0$ of degree $n>0$ with complex coefficients has a complex root.
items.intuition: Complex polynomials always have roots — proved topologically via winding numbers of loops, i.e. via $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.
items.label: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra
items.gloss: Complex polynomials always have roots — proved topologically via winding numbers of loops, i.e. via $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.
items.example: A non-vanishing polynomial would let a large loop be contracted, contradicting that its image winds $n$ times.

items.statement: If $X$ is compact and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then there exist $m,M\in X$ with $f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)$ for all $x\in X$.
items.formal_statement: If $X$ is compact and $f:X\to\mathbb R$ is continuous, then there exist $m,M\in X$ such that $f(m)\le f(x)\le f(M)$ for all $x\in X$.
items.intuition: A continuous real function on a compact space attains its maximum and minimum. The image is compact, hence closed and bounded, hence contains its sup and inf.
items.label: Generalized Extreme Value Theorem
items.gloss: A continuous real function on a compact space attains its maximum and minimum. The image is compact, hence closed and bounded, hence contains its sup and inf.
items.example: The classical “continuous functions on $[a,b]$ attain extrema” is the case $X=[a,b]$.
items.proof: By Theorem 6.37, $f (X)$ is compact. We must show that $f (X)$ contains its supremum and its infimum. If it does, then by setting $f (m) =$ inf $f (X)$ and $f (M) =$ sup $f (X)$ the theorem follows. We prove that $f (X)$ contains its supremum. The proof for the infimum is similar. Since $f (X)$ is compact, it is closed and bounded by Theorem 6.42. In particular, $f (X)$ is bounded above. Hence, the set $f (X)$ has a least upper bound; $s =$ sup $f (X)$. Thus $p \leq s$ for all $p \in f (X)$. We must show that $s \in f (X)$. Assume that $s \notin f (X)$. Since $f (X)$ is closed, i.e., $f (X)c = \mathbb{R}\setminus f (X)$ is open, it follows that there is a real number $\varepsilon > 0$ such that $(s - \varepsilon, s + \varepsilon) \subseteq f (X)c$, and so, $(s - \varepsilon, s + \varepsilon) \cap f (X) = \varnothing$. Hence, there is a real number $y$ such that $y$ is an upper bound of $f (X)$ and $s - \varepsilon < y < s$. This is a contradiction to the fact that $s$ is the least upper bound of $f (X)$. Hence, $s =$ sup $f (X) \in f (X)$.

proof_steps.label: Compact Image
proof_steps.content: By compactness preservation, $f(X)$ is compact in $\mathbb{R}$.

proof_steps.label: Closed and Bounded Image
proof_steps.content: By Heine-Borel in $\mathbb{R}$, $f(X)$ is closed and bounded, so it has a supremum and an infimum.

proof_steps.label: Supremum Is Attained
proof_steps.content: If $s=\sup f(X)$ were not in $f(X)$, closedness of $f(X)$ would give a neighborhood of $s$ disjoint from $f(X)$, producing a smaller upper bound and a contradiction.

proof_steps.label: Infimum Is Attained
proof_steps.content: The infimum is handled similarly. Thus $f$ attains both a minimum and a maximum on $X$.

items.label: Supremum (Least Upper Bound)
items.statement: For $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$, a number $s$ is the \textbf{supremum} of $A$ if $s$ is an upper bound for $A$ and every upper bound $u$ of $A$ satisfies $s\leq u$.
items.formal_statement: $$s=\sup A\Longleftrightarrow (\forall a\in A,\ a\leq s)\text{ and }(\forall u,\ (\forall a\in A,\ a\leq u)\Rightarrow s\leq u).$$
items.definition: The least upper bound of a subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
items.intuition: The supremum is the smallest ceiling above a set. In compactness arguments, closedness can force this ceiling to lie inside the set, turning a bound into an attained maximum.
assumptions: $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is nonempty and bounded above when existence is asserted.

items.statement: Let $X$ be connected and $f\colon X\to\mathbb{R}$ continuous. If $a,b\in X$ and $r$ lies between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then $f(c)=r$ for some $c\in X$.
items.intuition: A continuous real-valued function on a connected space hits every intermediate value — the classical IVT, freed from intervals.
items.label: Generalized Intermediate Value Theorem
items.gloss: A continuous real-valued function on a connected space hits every intermediate value — the classical IVT, freed from intervals.
items.example: If $r\notin f(X)$ then $f^{-1}((-\infty,r))$ and $f^{-1}((r,\infty))$ would separate $X$.
items.proof: Assume that $r \notin f (X)$. We will show that this contradicts the assumption that $X$ is connected. By the assumption that $r \notin f (X)$, i.e., $f (X) \subseteq \mathbb{R} \setminus \{r\} = (-\infty, r) \cup (r, \infty)$, we have a separation of $X: U = f^{-1} ((-\infty, r))$ and $V = f^{-1} ((r, \infty))$ are disjoint non-empty open subsets of $X$ whose union equals $X$. Thus they are a separation of $X$. This contradicts the assumption that $X$ is connected. Hence, $r \in f (X)$. In other words, there is a $c \in X$ such that $f (c) = r$.

proof_steps.label: Assume the Value Is Missing
proof_steps.content: Assume $r\notin f(X)$. Then $f(X)\subseteq (-\infty,r)\cup(r,\infty)$.

proof_steps.label: Pull Back the Two Rays
proof_steps.content: Set $U=f^{-1}((-\infty,r))$ and $V=f^{-1}((r,\infty))$. These are disjoint nonempty open sets whose union is $X$.

proof_steps.label: Contradict Connectedness
proof_steps.content: The pair $U,V$ separates $X$, contradicting connectedness. Hence $r\in f(X)$, so $f(c)=r$ for some $c\in X$.

items.statement: A \textbf{group} is a non-empty set $G$ with a binary operation $*$ such that:\;\textbf{(G1)}\ associativity $(a*b)*c=a*(b*c)$;\;\textbf{(G2)}\ an identity $e$ with $a*e=a=e*a$;\;\textbf{(G3)}\ inverses $a'$ with $a*a'=e=a'*a$. It is \textbf{abelian} if $*$ is commutative.
items.intuition: A set with an associative, invertible operation and a neutral element. The algebraic target of the fundamental group functor.
items.label: Group
items.gloss: A set with an associative, invertible operation and a neutral element. The algebraic target of the fundamental group functor.
items.example: $(\mathbb{Z},+)$ is an abelian group; $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is a group under $*$ (Thm. 7.16).

items.statement: A map $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$ is a \textbf{homomorphism} if $\varphi(x*_1 y)=\varphi(x)*_2\varphi(y)$ for all $x,y$.
items.intuition: A map respecting the group operations. Automatically $\varphi(e_1)=e_2$ and $\varphi(x^{-1})=\varphi(x)^{-1}$. Composites of homomorphisms are homomorphisms.
items.label: Group Homomorphism
items.gloss: A map respecting the group operations. Automatically $\varphi(e_1)=e_2$ and $\varphi(x^{-1})=\varphi(x)^{-1}$. Composites of homomorphisms are homomorphisms.
items.example: The induced map $h_*\colon\pi_1(X)\to\pi_1(Y)$ is a homomorphism (Def. 7.22).

items.statement: For a continuous bijection $f\colon X\to Y$, the following are equivalent: (1) $f$ is a homeomorphism; (2) $f$ is an open map; (3) $f$ is a closed map.
items.intuition: For a continuous bijection, having a continuous inverse is the same as being open, which is the same as being closed.
items.label: Homeo ⇔ Open ⇔ Closed (Bijective)
items.gloss: For a continuous bijection, having a continuous inverse is the same as being open, which is the same as being closed.
items.example: Often the quickest route to proving a continuous bijection is a homeomorphism (e.g. from a compact space to a Hausdorff one).
items.proof: We will show that (1) is equivalent to (2) and that (2) is equivalent to (3). Since $f$ is a bijection, we have ( $-1 )^{-1} f (U ) = f (U )$ for any subset $U$ of $X$. In particular, if $U$ is an open subset of $X$ and if we assume that $f$ is a homeomorphism then $f (U )$ must be open in $Y$ as $f^{-1}$ is continuous. Hence, $f$ is an open map. Furthermore, if we assume that $f$ is open instead of being a homeomorphism then $f (U )$ is open in $Y$ for $U$ an open subset of $X$. Hence, $f^{-1}: Y \to X$ is continuous. Thus $f$ is a homeomorphism. Hence, (1) is equivalent to (2). Assume that $f$ is an open map. Since $f$ is a bijection, we have $f (X \setminus U ) = f (U^{c} ) = f (U )c = Y \setminus f (U )$. Thus $f$ is closed: $X \setminus U$ is closed and $Y \setminus f (U )$ is closed. The opposite implication follows by a completely analogous argument. Hence, (2) is equivalent to (3). This completes the proof.

proof_steps.label: Homeomorphism Implies Open
proof_steps.content: If $f$ is a homeomorphism and $U\subseteq X$ is open, then $f(U)=(f^{-1})^{-1}(U)$ is open in $Y$ because $f^{-1}$ is continuous.

proof_steps.label: Open Bijection Implies Homeomorphism
proof_steps.content: If $f$ is a bijective open continuous map, then for every open $U\subseteq X$, $f(U)$ is open, which says $f^{-1}:Y\to X$ is continuous. Hence $f$ is a homeomorphism.

proof_steps.label: Open and Closed Are Equivalent for Bijections
proof_steps.content: For a bijection, $f(X\setminus U)=Y\setminus f(U)$. Therefore preserving open sets is equivalent to preserving closed sets.

items.statement: Continuous maps $f_0,f_1\colon X\to Y$ are \textbf{homotopic} ($f_0\simeq f_1$) if there is a continuous $H\colon X\times I\to Y$ with $H(x,0)=f_0(x)$ and $H(x,1)=f_1(x)$. $H$ is a \textbf{homotopy}.
items.intuition: A continuous one-parameter deformation of one map into another. Think of $t\in[0,1]$ as time, sliding $f_0$ to $f_1$.
items.label: Homotopy
items.gloss: A continuous one-parameter deformation of one map into another. Think of $t\in[0,1]$ as time, sliding $f_0$ to $f_1$.
items.example: If $f_0\simeq f_1$ with $f_1$ constant, $f_0$ is \emph{nullhomotopic}. Any two maps into $\mathbb{R}^n$ are homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

items.statement: On $C(X,Y)$ (continuous maps $X\to Y$), $\simeq$ is an equivalence relation. Its classes are \textbf{homotopy classes}; $[f]$ denotes the class of $f$ and $[X,Y]=C(X,Y)/\!\simeq$.
items.intuition: Bundle maps that can be deformed into one another. Topology often studies $[X,Y]$ rather than individual maps.
items.label: Homotopy Classes
items.gloss: Bundle maps that can be deformed into one another. Topology often studies $[X,Y]$ rather than individual maps.
items.example: $[X,\mathbb{R}^n]$ has a single element, since all such maps are homotopic.

items.statement: Maps $f\colon X\to Y$, $g\colon Y\to X$ are \textbf{homotopy equivalences} if $g\circ f\simeq\mathrm{id}_X$ and $f\circ g\simeq\mathrm{id}_Y$; each is a \textbf{homotopy inverse} of the other.
items.intuition: A weaker sameness than homeomorphism: the spaces are interchangeable up to deformation. Every homeomorphism is a homotopy equivalence, but not conversely.
items.label: Homotopy Equivalence
items.gloss: A weaker sameness than homeomorphism: the spaces are interchangeable up to deformation. Every homeomorphism is a homotopy equivalence, but not conversely.
items.example: $\mathbb{R}$ and a point $\{p\}$ are homotopy equivalent but not homeomorphic.

items.statement: If $f\colon X\to Y$ is a homotopy equivalence with $f(x_0)=y_0$, then $f_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
items.intuition: $\pi_1$ is an invariant of homotopy type, not merely of homeomorphism type — a stronger and very useful fact.
items.label: Homotopy Equivalence Induces Iso
items.gloss: $\pi_1$ is an invariant of homotopy type, not merely of homeomorphism type — a stronger and very useful fact.
items.example: Lets you compute $\pi_1$ of a space via any simpler space it deformation retracts onto.
items.proof: Let $g: Y \to X$ be a homotopy inverse of $f$ with $g(y_0 ) = x_1$, and let $f (x_1 ) = y_1$. By assumption, we have $g\circ f \simeq$ id$X$ and $f \circ g \simeq$ id$Y$ where id$X: X \to X$ and id$Y: Y \to Y$ are the identity maps of $X$ and $Y$, respectively. Thus, by Lemma 7.37, there is a path $\alpha: I \to X$ in $X$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and a path $\beta: I \to Y$ from $y_0$ to $y_1$ such that $(g\circ(f_{x 0} ))* = g* \circ(f_{x 0} )* = \alpha \circ$ (id$X )* = \alpha$ and $\hat{Y} )* = \hat{\beta} ((f_{x 1} )\circ g)* = (f_{x 1} )* \circ g* = \beta\circ$ (id where we have specified the basepoint for the homomorphisms induced by $f$. In other words, the following two diagrams commute. (id$X )* g* \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \pi_1 (Y, y_0 ) \pi_1 (X, x_1 ) \alpha (\hat{f}_{x 1} )* g* \circ(f_{x 0} )* \hat{\beta} \pi_1 (X, x_1 ) \pi_1 (Y, y_1 )$ Since $\hat{\alpha}$ and $\hat{\beta}$ are both isomorphisms, it follows by commutativity of the diagram on the left that $g*$ is an epimorphism, and by commutativity of the diagram on the right that $g*$ is a monomorphism. Hence, $g*$ is an isomorphism. Since $(f_{x 0} )* = (g* )^{-1} \circ \hat{\alpha}$ it follows that $(f_{x 0} )*$ is an isomorphism.

items.statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. Any continuous $H\colon I\times I\to B$ with $H(0,0)=b_0$ has a unique lift $\tilde H\colon I\times I\to E$ with $\tilde H(0,0)=e_0$; if $H$ is a path homotopy so is $\tilde H$.
items.intuition: Whole homotopies lift uniquely, and path homotopies lift to path homotopies. This makes the lifting correspondence well-defined on $\pi_1$.
items.label: Homotopy Lifting Property
items.gloss: Whole homotopies lift uniquely, and path homotopies lift to path homotopies. This makes the lifting correspondence well-defined on $\pi_1$.
items.example: Ensures path-homotopic loops in $B$ have lifts with the same endpoint, so $\Phi$ is well-defined (Def. 8.14).
items.proof: We prove the statement about path homotopies. The proof of the first part of the theorem is similar to the proof of Theorem 8.12, where we make use of Lemma 8.10 to break $I \times I$ into smaller squares $[s_{i-1}, s_i ] \times [t_{i-1}, t_i ]$ that are mapped by $H$ into open sets of $B$ that are evenly covered by $p$. See [4, pp. 343–344] for details. Assume that $H$ is a path homotopy from the path $f_0: I \to B$ to the path $f_1: I \to B$ where $f_0 (0) = f_1 (0) = b_0$ and $f_0 (1) = f_1 (1) = b_1$. Then the map $H_0: I \to B$ given by $H_0 (t) = H(0, t) 0: \tilde{I} \to E$ given by is the constant path in $B$ at $b_0$. Hence, the map $H 0 (\tilde{t}) = H(0, H \tilde{t})$ is a lift of $H_0$ where $H 0 (0) = \tilde{H}(0, 0) = e_0$. Note that the constant path $c_{e 0}: I \to E$ at $e_0$ is also a lift of $H_0$ such that $c_{e 0} (0) = e_0$. Thus by uniqueness $H 0 = \tilde{c}_e$. In other words, $\tilde{H}(0, t) = e_0$ for all $t \in I$. Similarly, let $H_1: I \to B$ be the map given by $H_1 (t) = H(1, t). 0) = \tilde{e}_1$. Then the map $H$ Then $H_1$ is the constant path $c_{b 1}$ in $B$ at $b_1$. Let $H(1, 1: \tilde{I} \to E$ given by $1 (\tilde{t}) = \tilde{H} H(1, t)$ is a lift of $H_1$ where $H 1 (0) = \tilde{H}(1, 0) = \tilde{e}_1$. As the constant path $c_e: I \to E$ at $e_1$ is also a lift of $1 = \tilde{c}_e$. In other words, $H(1, H_1$ such that $c_{e 1} (0) = e_1$, it follows by uniqueness that $H \tilde{t}) = e_1$ for all $t \in I$. Thus $H$ is a path homotopy from $\tilde{f}_0$ to $\tilde{f}_1$.

items.statement: Spaces $X,Y$ have the same \textbf{homotopy type} (are \textbf{homotopy equivalent}) if there is a homotopy equivalence $f\colon X\to Y$.
items.intuition: The equivalence relation under which topology classifies spaces ‘up to deformation’. Many invariants, including $\pi_1$, depend only on homotopy type.
items.label: Homotopy Type
items.gloss: The equivalence relation under which topology classifies spaces ‘up to deformation’. Many invariants, including $\pi_1$, depend only on homotopy type.
items.example: All contractible spaces (those homotopy equivalent to a point) share one homotopy type.

items.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $A\subseteq X$, the image of $A$ is $f(A)=\{f(x)\mid x\in A\}$. The image of $f$ is $f(X)$.
items.formal_statement: $f(A)=\{y\in Y:\exists x\in A,\ f(x)=y\}$.
items.definition: The subset of the codomain attained by applying a function to a subset of the domain.
items.intuition: What the function actually hits.
items.label: Image of a Map
items.gloss: All values actually attained by $f$. $f$ is surjective exactly when $f(A)=B$.
items.example: For $f\colon\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$, $f(x)=x^2$, the image is $[0,\infty)$.

items.statement: For $A\subseteq X$, the inclusion map $i:A\hookrightarrow X$ is $i(a)=a$.
items.formal_statement: $i:A\to X$, $i(a)=a$.
items.definition: The canonical function viewing a subset as part of its ambient set.
items.intuition: It does nothing to points; it only changes the ambient viewpoint.
items.label: Inclusion Map
items.gloss: The map that views a subset as sitting inside the ambient set. Composing with it recovers a restriction: $f\circ i=f|_R$.
items.example: The inclusion of a subspace is continuous for the subspace topology.

items.statement: On any set $X$, the \textbf{indiscrete topology} is $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}=\{\varnothing,X\}$.
items.intuition: The coarsest possible topology — the only open sets are the empty set and the whole space. Points are topologically indistinguishable.
items.label: Indiscrete Topology
items.gloss: The coarsest possible topology — the only open sets are the empty set and the whole space. Points are topologically indistinguishable.
items.example: For any topology $\mathcal{T}$ on $X$ we have $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\subseteq\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$. Every map \emph{into} an indiscrete space is continuous.

items.statement: A based map $h\colon(X,x_0)\to(Y,y_0)$ induces $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ by $h_*([f])=[h\circ f]$; it is a group homomorphism (Lemma 7.23).
items.intuition: Maps of spaces turn into maps of fundamental groups by post-composing loops. This is how $\pi_1$ becomes algebraic.
items.label: Induced Homomorphism
items.gloss: Maps of spaces turn into maps of fundamental groups by post-composing loops. This is how $\pi_1$ becomes algebraic.
items.example: $(\mathrm{id}_X)_*$ is the identity; composites behave well (functoriality, Thm. 7.24).

items.statement: $A\cap B=\{x\mid x\in A\text{ and }x\in B\}$ and $A\cup B=\{x\mid x\in A\text{ or }x\in B\}$. Arbitrary unions and finite intersections are part of the definition of a topology.
items.formal_statement: $\bigcup_{i\in I}A_i=\{x:\exists i\in I, x\in A_i\}$ and $\bigcap_{i\in I}A_i=\{x:\forall i\in I, x\in A_i\}$.
items.definition: Set operations that combine collections by pooling elements or retaining common elements.
items.intuition: Union glues open pieces together; intersection checks simultaneous membership.
items.label: Intersection and Union
items.gloss: Intersection keeps common elements; union pools both. They are commutative, associative, and distribute over each other.
items.example: $A,B$ are \emph{disjoint} when $A\cap B=\varnothing$. Always $A\cap B\subseteq A\subseteq A\cup B$.

items.statement: If $\mathcal{T}_1,\mathcal{T}_2$ are topologies on $X$, then $\mathcal{T}_1\cap\mathcal{T}_2$ is a topology. More generally $\bigcap_{\lambda}\mathcal{T}_\lambda$ is a topology for any family.
items.intuition: Topologies are closed under intersection — the basis for “generating” the smallest topology containing a given collection. The \emph{union} of topologies generally is not a topology.
items.label: Intersection of Topologies
items.gloss: Topologies are closed under intersection — the basis for “generating” the smallest topology containing a given collection. The \emph{union} of topologies generally is not a topology.
items.example: On $\{a,b,c\}$, intersecting two topologies can give $\{\varnothing,X\}$ while their union fails T2.
items.proof: Clearly, $\varnothing$ and $X$ are in $\mathcal{T}_1 \cap \mathcal{T}_2$, so T1 is satisfied. Let $\{U_\lambda \}\lambda\in\Lambda$ be a collection of sets such that $U_\lambda \in \mathcal{T}_1 \cap \mathcal{T}_2$ for each $\lambda \in \Lambda$ where $\Lambda$ is some $\bigcup$

items.statement: An \textbf{isomorphism} is a bijective homomorphism $\varphi\colon G_1\to G_2$. An isomorphism from a group to itself is an \textbf{automorphism}.
items.intuition: A perfect structural match between groups: same group, relabelled. Isomorphic groups are interchangeable for all group-theoretic purposes.
items.label: Isomorphism
items.gloss: A perfect structural match between groups: same group, relabelled. Isomorphic groups are interchangeable for all group-theoretic purposes.
items.example: $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is an isomorphism (Thm. 8.16).

items.statement: For a compact metric space $(X,d)$ and an open cover $\mathcal{A}$, there is $\lambda>0$ (a \textbf{Lebesgue number}) such that every ball $\mathsf{B}(x;\lambda)$ lies inside some single $U\in\mathcal{A}$.
items.intuition: On a compact metric space, every open cover has a uniform scale below which small balls fit inside one cover element. Enables subdividing paths finely enough to lift.
items.label: Lebesgue Number Lemma
items.gloss: On a compact metric space, every open cover has a uniform scale below which small balls fit inside one cover element. Enables subdividing paths finely enough to lift.
items.example: Used to chop $I$ into small subintervals, each mapping into one evenly covered open set (proof of Thm. 8.12).
items.proof: If $X \in \mathcal{A}$, then every real number $\lambda > 0$ will be a Lebesgue number for $\mathcal{A}$. So assume that $X$ is not an element of $\mathcal{A}$. By compactness, there is a finite subcollection, say, $\{U_1, U_2, \ldots, U_n \}$ of $\mathcal{A}$ that covers $X$. For each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$, let $V_i = X \setminus U_i$. Then each $V_i$ is non-empty as $U_i \neq X$ for all $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Let $\mathbb{R}$ denote the set of real numbers equipped with the standard topology (and the standard Euclidean metric), and let $f: X \to \mathbb{R}$ be given by $n 1\sum f (x) =$ dist $V_i (x) n i=1$ where dist $V_i: X \to \mathbb{R}$ is given by dist $V_i (x) =$ inf $\{d(x, v) \mid v \in V_i \}$. We claim that dist $V_i$ is continuous for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be two points in $X$. Then by M3, we have dist $V_i (x_1 ) \leq d(x_1, v) \leq d(x_1, x_2 ) + d(x_2, v)$, for all $v \in V_i$, and so, dist $V_i (x_1 ) - d(x_1, x_2 ) \leq$ dist $V_i (x_2 )$. Similarly, we have dist $V_i (x_2 ) - d(x_1, x_2 ) \leq$ dist $V_i (x_1 )$. Hence, | dist $V_i (x_1 ) -$ dist $V_i (x_2 )| \leq d(x_1, x_2 )$. Thus dist $V_i$ is continuous for each $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$. Hence, $f$ is also continuous.

items.statement: For $H\le G$ and $g\in G$: the \textbf{left coset} is $gH=\{g*h\mid h\in H\}$ and the \textbf{right coset} is $Hg=\{h*g\mid h\in H\}$.
items.intuition: Translates of a subgroup. Cosets partition $G$ into equal-sized blocks; they coincide ($gH=Hg$) precisely for normal subgroups.
items.label: Left and Right Cosets
items.gloss: Translates of a subgroup. Cosets partition $G$ into equal-sized blocks; they coincide ($gH=Hg$) precisely for normal subgroups.
items.example: In an abelian group all left and right cosets agree.

items.statement: Given $p\colon E\to B$ and $f\colon X\to B$, a \textbf{lifting} of $f$ is a continuous $\tilde f\colon X\to E$ with $p\circ\tilde f=f$.
items.intuition: Lift a map from the base into the total space, compatibly with $p$. Path and homotopy lifting (for covering maps) drive the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.
items.label: Lifting
items.gloss: Lift a map from the base into the total space, compatibly with $p$. Path and homotopy lifting (for covering maps) drive the computation of $\pi_1(S^1)$.
items.example: A path in $S^1$ lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ once a starting point is chosen (Thm. 8.12).

items.statement: For a covering $p\colon E\to B$ with $p(e_0)=b_0$, the \textbf{lifting correspondence} is $\Phi\colon\pi_1(B,b_0)\to p^{-1}(b_0)$, $\Phi([f])=\tilde f(1)$, where $\tilde f$ is the lift of the loop $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
items.intuition: Send each loop class to the endpoint of its lift in the fibre. Well-defined by homotopy lifting; a bijection when $E$ is path-connected (Thm. 8.15).
items.label: Lifting Correspondence
items.gloss: Send each loop class to the endpoint of its lift in the fibre. Well-defined by homotopy lifting; a bijection when $E$ is path-connected (Thm. 8.15).
items.example: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, $\Phi$ sends a loop to its winding number, giving $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

items.statement: $f\colon X\to Y$ is a \textbf{local homeomorphism} if every $x\in X$ has a neighborhood $U$ with $f(U)$ open and $f|_U\colon U\to f(U)$ a homeomorphism.
items.intuition: Looks like a homeomorphism in a neighborhood of each point, though not necessarily globally. Every covering map is a local homeomorphism.
items.label: Local Homeomorphism
items.gloss: Looks like a homeomorphism in a neighborhood of each point, though not necessarily globally. Every covering map is a local homeomorphism.
items.example: Preserves local properties such as local connectedness; $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$ is a local homeomorphism but not injective.

items.statement: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space, let $x\in X$, and let $A\subseteq X$. $A$ is a \textbf{neighborhood} of $x$ if it contains some open ball centered at $x$. It is an \textbf{open neighborhood} of $x$ if, in addition, $A$ is open.
items.formal_statement: $$\begin{aligned} A\in\mathcal N_X(x) &\Longleftrightarrow \exists\varepsilon>0\text{ such that }B_d(x,\varepsilon)\subseteq A,\\ B_d(x,\varepsilon) &=\{y\in X\mid d(x,y)<\varepsilon\}. \end{aligned}$$
items.definition: In a metric space $(X,d)$, a subset $A\subseteq X$ is a neighborhood of $x\in X$ if there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that the open ball $B_d(x,\varepsilon)$ is contained in $A$.
items.intuition: A neighborhood of $x$ is any set with positive metric breathing room around $x$. The set itself need not be open; it only has to contain a small open ball centered at $x$.
items.label: Neighborhood (Metric)
items.gloss: Metric version of the broad neighborhood convention: $A$ may be non-open, provided it contains some open ball around $x$. If $A$ itself is open, it is an open neighborhood.
items.example: In $\mathbb R$, $[-1,2]$ is a neighborhood of $0$ because $(-1/2,1/2)\subseteq[-1,2]$. It is not a neighborhood of $2$, since no open interval centered at $2$ lies inside $[-1,2]$.
equivalent_definitions: topological neighborhood in the topology induced by the metric
items.convention_notes: Uses the broad neighborhood convention. Some texts reserve 'neighborhood' for open neighborhoods; in that stricter convention this node describes a set that contains an open neighborhood of the point.

convention_review.selected_meaning: A metric neighborhood of x is any subset A containing an open ball B_d(x,epsilon). A itself need not be open.
alternative_conventions: Some authors require neighborhoods themselves to be open; then the broad version is called a neighborhood in the weak sense or a set containing an open neighborhood.
convention_review.modeling_decision: Keep this node as the metric characterization of the broad topological neighborhood and relate it to neighborhood and open_neighborhood.
convention_review.exam_warning: Check whether the course notes use 'neighborhood' broadly or mean 'open neighborhood'. State the convention if there is any doubt.

items.statement: Let $(X,\mathcal{T})$ be a topological space and let $U\subseteq X$. Then $$\begin{aligned} U\in\mathcal{T} &\iff \forall x\in U,\ \exists U_x\in\mathcal{T}\text{ such that }x\in U_x\subseteq U \\ &\iff U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x\text{ for some choice of open sets }U_x\in\mathcal{T}\text{ with }x\in U_x\subseteq U. \end{aligned}$$ Equivalently, $U$ is open exactly when every point of $U$ has an open neighborhood contained in $U$.
items.intuition: A set is open exactly when every point inside it has some open neighborhood still contained in the set. If $U$ is already open, choose $U_x=U$ for every $x\in U$. Conversely, if such neighborhoods $U_x$ exist, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$, so $U$ is open.
items.label: Neighborhood Characterization of Open Sets
