items.gloss: A set is open exactly when every point inside it has some open neighborhood still contained in the set. If $U$ is already open, choose $U_x=U$ for every $x\in U$. Conversely, if such neighborhoods $U_x$ exist, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$, so $U$ is open.
items.example: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, $(0,1)$ is open because every point $x\in(0,1)$ has a smaller open interval around it contained in $(0,1)$. The set $[0,1]$ is not open in $\mathbb{R}$ because $0$ has no open neighborhood contained in $[0,1]$.
items.proof: Assume that $U$ is open in $X$. Then for every $x \in U, U$ is a neighborhood of $x$ that is contained in $U$. We prove the other implication. Assume that for every $x \in U$ there is a $U_x \in \mathcal{T}$ such that $x \in U_x \subseteq U$, i.e., that $U_x$ is a neighborhood of $x$ such that $U_x \subseteq U$. To prove that $U \in \mathcal{T}$, we will $\bigcup \bigcup$ prove that $U = x\in U U_x$. Assume that $x' \in U_x'$. Then $x' \in U_x' \subseteq x\in U U_x$. Furthermore, any $\bigcup$ point in $x\in U U_x$ is in $U_x$ for some $x \in U$ so by assumption, $U_x \subseteq U$ and $x \in U_x \subseteq U$. Hence, $\bigcup U = x\in U U_x$. As $U$ is the union of open sets it must be an open set as well by T2.

proof_steps.label: Open Sets Give Neighborhoods
proof_steps.content: If $U$ is open and $x\in U$, then $U$ itself is an open neighborhood of $x$ contained in $U$.

proof_steps.label: Neighborhoods Cover U
proof_steps.content: Conversely, if every $x\in U$ lies in an open set $U_x\subseteq U$, then $U=\bigcup_{x\in U}U_x$.

proof_steps.label: Union Is Open
proof_steps.content: Since arbitrary unions of open sets are open, $U$ is open.

items.statement: $H\le G$ is \textbf{normal} if $gH=Hg$ for all $g$; equivalently $g*h*g^{-1}\in H$ for all $g\in G$, $h\in H$.
items.intuition: A subgroup whose left and right cosets agree — exactly the condition needed for the cosets to form a group.
items.label: Normal Subgroup
items.gloss: A subgroup whose left and right cosets agree — exactly the condition needed for the cosets to form a group.
items.example: Every subgroup of an abelian group is normal; kernels of homomorphisms are always normal.

items.statement: A map $f$ is \textbf{nullhomotopic} if $f\simeq c$ for some constant map $c$.
items.intuition: The map can be continuously shrunk to a single point. Loops being nullhomotopic is what makes a space simply connected.
items.label: Nullhomotopic Map
items.gloss: The map can be continuously shrunk to a single point. Loops being nullhomotopic is what makes a space simply connected.
items.example: Every map into $\mathbb{R}^n$ is nullhomotopic; the identity $S^1\to S^1$ is \emph{not}.

items.statement: $A\subseteq X$ is \textbf{open} if every $a\in A$ has an open ball $\mathsf{B}(a;r)\subseteq A$. It is \textbf{closed} if its complement $A^c=X\setminus A$ is open.
items.intuition: Open means every point sits inside the set with a little room to spare. Closed is defined by its complement. Most sets are neither; sets that are both are called \emph{clopen}.
items.label: Open / Closed Set (Metric)
items.gloss: Open means every point sits inside the set with a little room to spare. Closed is defined by its complement. Most sets are neither; sets that are both are called \emph{clopen}.
items.example: Open balls are open and closed balls are closed (Lemma 2.10). Both $\varnothing$ and $X$ are clopen.

items.statement: For $(X,d)$, $a\in X$ and $r>0$: the \textbf{open ball} is $\mathsf{B}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)<r\}$ and the \textbf{closed ball} is $\overline{\mathsf{B}}(a;r)=\{x\in X\mid d(x,a)\le r\}$.
items.intuition: All points within distance $r$ of a centre $a$ — strictly less than $r$ for open, at most $r$ for closed. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ these are ordinary balls and discs.
items.label: Open and Closed Balls
items.gloss: All points within distance $r$ of a centre $a$ — strictly less than $r$ for open, at most $r$ for closed. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ these are ordinary balls and discs.
items.example: In the discrete metric, $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=\{x\}$ for $r\le 1$ and $\mathsf{B}(x;r)=X$ for $r>1$.

items.statement: A continuous $f\colon X\to Y$ is an \textbf{open map} if $f(U)$ is open for every open $U$, and a \textbf{closed map} if $f(V)$ is closed for every closed $V$.
items.intuition: These push openness/closedness \emph{forward}, unlike continuity which pulls openness back. They are the tool linking quotient maps to homeomorphisms.
items.label: Open and Closed Maps
items.gloss: These push openness/closedness \emph{forward}, unlike continuity which pulls openness back. They are the tool linking quotient maps to homeomorphisms.
items.example: Every homeomorphism is both open and closed, but $\mathbb{R}\to\{*\}$ is open and closed yet not a homeomorphism.

items.statement: Let $X=A\cup B$ with $A,B$ closed in $X$, and let $f\colon A\to Y$, $g\colon B\to Y$ be continuous with $f=g$ on $A\cap B$. Then $h\colon X\to Y$ defined piecewise by $f$ and $g$ is continuous.
items.intuition: Two continuous pieces that agree on the overlap glue to a continuous whole — provided the pieces are closed.
items.label: Pasting Lemma
items.gloss: Two continuous pieces that agree on the overlap glue to a continuous whole — provided the pieces are closed.
items.example: Makes the concatenation of paths $f*g$ continuous (Def. 7.12).
items.proof: We will prove that the preimage of a closed subset of $Y$ under $h$ is closed in $X$. Let $C$ be a closed subset of $Y$. Then $h^{-1} (C) = f^{-1} (C) \cup g^{-1} (C)$. Since $f$ is continuous, it follows that $f^{-1} (C)$ is closed in $A$, and so, it is closed in $X$. Similarly, $g^{-1} (C)$ is closed in $B$, and so, it is closed in $X$. Hence, $h^{-1} (C) = f^{-1} (C) \cup g^{-1} (C)$ is closed in $X$ as it is the union of two closed subsets of $X$. Thus $h$ is continuous.

proof_steps.label: Use Closed-Set Continuity
proof_steps.content: To prove $h$ is continuous, test preimages of closed subsets of $Y$. Let $C\subseteq Y$ be closed.

proof_steps.label: Split the Preimage
proof_steps.content: Because $h$ restricts to $f$ on $A$ and to $g$ on $B$, one has $h^{-1}(C)=f^{-1}(C)\cup g^{-1}(C)$.

proof_steps.label: Use Closed Pieces
proof_steps.content: The sets $f^{-1}(C)$ and $g^{-1}(C)$ are closed in the closed pieces $A$ and $B$, hence closed in $X$. Their finite union is closed, so $h$ is continuous.

items.statement: Paths $f,g\colon I\to X$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ are \textbf{path homotopic} ($f\simeq_p g$) if there is continuous $H\colon I\times I\to X$ with $H(s,0)=f(s)$, $H(s,1)=g(s)$, and $H(0,t)=x_0$, $H(1,t)=x_1$ for all $t$.
items.intuition: Deform one path into another while keeping the endpoints pinned. This rel-endpoints relation is what the fundamental group is built from.
items.label: Path Homotopy
items.gloss: Deform one path into another while keeping the endpoints pinned. This rel-endpoints relation is what the fundamental group is built from.
items.example: Any two paths in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with the same endpoints are path homotopic via the straight-line homotopy.

items.statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$, $[f]=\{g\mid g\ \text{a path}\ x_0\to x_1,\ f\simeq_p g\}$ is its \textbf{path homotopy class}.
items.intuition: The endpoint-fixed deformation class of a path. The fundamental group consists of such classes for loops.
items.label: Path Homotopy Classes
items.gloss: The endpoint-fixed deformation class of a path. The fundamental group consists of such classes for loops.
items.example: The product $*$ and inverse descend to these classes, giving group structure (Thm. 7.14).

items.statement: Every path-connected space is connected.
items.intuition: Being walkable implies being in one piece. The converse fails in general, though it holds for open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$.
items.label: Path-Connected ⇒ Connected
items.gloss: Being walkable implies being in one piece. The converse fails in general, though it holds for open subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$.
items.example: The topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected (a standard counterexample).
items.proof: Assume that $X$ is path-connected but that $X$ is disconnected, i.e., there is a separation of $X$. Let $U$ and $V$ be a separation of $X$. Let $f: [a, b] \to X$ be a path from $x \in U$ to $y \in V$. Then $f^{-1} (U )$ and $f^{-1} (V )$ are disjoint non-empty open subsets of $[a, b]$ whose union is equal to $[a, b]$. Thus they are a separation of $[a, b]$. This is a contradiction to the fact that $[a, b]$ is a connected space.

proof_steps.label: Assume a Separation
proof_steps.content: Assume $X$ is path-connected but disconnected, and let $U,V$ be a separation of $X$.

proof_steps.label: Choose a Path Across the Separation
proof_steps.content: Pick $x\in U$ and $y\in V$. Path-connectedness gives a path $f:[a,b]\to X$ from $x$ to $y$.

proof_steps.label: Separate the Interval
proof_steps.content: The sets $f^{-1}(U)$ and $f^{-1}(V)$ are disjoint nonempty open subsets whose union is $[a,b]$, contradicting connectedness of intervals.

items.statement: If $X_1,\dots,X_n$ are compact, then $X_1\times\cdots\times X_n$ is compact.
items.intuition: Compactness survives finite products (and, by Tychonoff, arbitrary products with the product topology).
items.label: Product of Compact Spaces Is Compact
items.gloss: Compactness survives finite products (and, by Tychonoff, arbitrary products with the product topology).
items.example: With Thm. 6.40 this gives compactness of closed boxes in $\mathbb{R}^n$, hence Heine–Borel.
items.proof: We prove the statement for the product of two compact spaces. The general result then follows by an inductive argument. Let $X$ and $Y$ be compact spaces. Let $\mathcal{A}$ be an open cover of $X \times Y$. We must show that there is a finite subcover $\mathcal{A}'$ of $\mathcal{A}$. For each $x \in X, \{x\} \times Y$ is compact as it is homeomorphic to $Y$ which is assumed to be compact. Thus there is a finite subcollection $\mathcal{A}_x$ of $\mathcal{A}$ that covers $\{x\} \times Y$. Let $\bigcup U_x = A_x \in\mathcal{A}_x A_x$. Then $U_x$ is open in $X \times Y$ and contains $\{x\} \times Y$. Thus by Lemma 6.38 for each $x \in X$ there is a neighborhood $W_x \subseteq X$ such that $x \in W_x$ and $W_x \times Y \subseteq U_x$. Furthermore, $\mathcal{A}_x$ covers $W_x \times Y$. Now let $x \in X$ vary. The collection $\{W_x \}x\in X$ is then an open cover of $X$. Since $X$ is compact, there must be a finite subcover $\{W_{x 1}, W_{x 2}, \ldots, W_{x n} \}$ of $\{W_x \}x\in X$. For each 1 $\leq i \leq n$ the subspace $W_{x i} \times Y$ is covered by the finite subcollection $\mathcal{A}_{x i}$ of $\mathcal{A}$. Hence, $n \bigcup X\times Y = W_x i \times Y i=1 \bigcup n$ is covered by the subcollection $\mathcal{A}' = i=1 \mathcal{A}_{x i}$ of $\mathcal{A}$. Thus $X \times Y$ is compact.

proof_steps.label: Reduce to Two Factors
proof_steps.content: It suffices to prove the two-factor case; the finite product case follows by induction.

proof_steps.label: Cover Each Vertical Slice
proof_steps.content: For an open cover of $X\times Y$, each slice $\{x\}\times Y$ is compact and therefore is covered by finitely many cover elements.

proof_steps.label: Thicken the Slice
proof_steps.content: The tube lemma gives a neighborhood $W_x$ of $x$ such that $W_x\times Y$ is covered by the same finite subcollection.

proof_steps.label: Use Compactness of X
proof_steps.content: The neighborhoods $W_x$ cover $X$. Choose finitely many using compactness of $X$; the corresponding finite cover elements cover all of $X\times Y$.

items.canonical_label: Product of compact spaces is compact
items.display_label: Product of Compact Spaces Is Compact

items.statement: For a path $f$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and $g$ from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the \textbf{product} $f*g\colon I\to X$ is $f$ run on $[0,\tfrac12]$ then $g$ on $[\tfrac12,1]$: $(f*g)(s)=f(2s)$ for $s\le\tfrac12$, $g(2s-1)$ otherwise.
items.intuition: Concatenate two paths by traversing the first at double speed, then the second. Continuous by the pasting lemma.
items.label: Product of Paths
items.gloss: Concatenate two paths by traversing the first at double speed, then the second. Continuous by the pasting lemma.
items.example: On path homotopy classes, $*$ is associative with constant-path units and reverse-path inverses (Thm. 7.14).

items.statement: For a normal subgroup $H\trianglelefteq G$, the cosets $G/H$ form a group under $(g_1H)(g_2H)=(g_1g_2)H$.
items.intuition: Collapse a normal subgroup to the identity: the cosets inherit a well-defined group structure. The algebraic analogue of a quotient space.
items.label: Quotient (Factor) Group
items.gloss: Collapse a normal subgroup to the identity: the cosets inherit a well-defined group structure. The algebraic analogue of a quotient space.
items.example: $\mathbb{Z}/m\mathbb{Z}$ is the cyclic group of order $m$.
items.proof: We first show that the binary operation defined on $G/H$ is well-defined. Let $g_1' \in g_1 H$, and let $g_2' \in g_2 H$. Then $g_1 H = g_1' H$ and $g_2 H = g_2' H$. We must show that $(g_1 H)(g_2 H) = (g_1' H)(g_2' H)$. Choose an element $(g_1 g_2 )h \in (g_1 g_2 )H$. Since $g_1 \in g_1 H = g_1' H$ and $g_2 \in g_2 H = g_2' H$, there are elements $h_1$ and $h_2$ in $H$ such that $g_1 = g_1' h_1$ and $g_2 = g_2' h_2$. Thus $(g_1 g_2 )h = (g_1' h_1 )(g_2' h_2 )h = g_1' h_1 g_2' h_2 h = g_1' (g_2' (g_2' )^{-1} )h_1 g_2' h_2 h = g_1' g_2' ((g_2' )^{-1} h_1 g_2' )h_2 h = g_1' g_2' h'1 h_2 h \in (g_1' g_2' )H$ Appendix B. Elementary algebra 105 where the last equality follows from the fact $H$ is a normal subgroup of $G$; there is an $h'1 \in H$ such that $(g_2' )^{-1} h_1 g_2 = h'1$. Hence, $(g_1 g_2 )H \subseteq (g_1' g_2' )H$. A similar argument shows that $(g_1' g_2' )H \subseteq (g_1 g_2 )H$. Hence, $(g_1 g_2 )H = (g_1' g_2' )H$. Thus $(g_1 H)(g_2 H) = (g_1 g_2 )H = (g_1' g_2' )H = (g_1' H)(g_2' H)$. We now show that $G/H$ is a group. Let $g_1 H, g_2 H$ and $g_2 H$ be elements in $G/H$. We must show that $[(g_1 H)(g_2 H)](g_3 H) = (g_1 H)[(g_2 H)(g_3 H)]$. By definition, we have $[(g_1 H)(g_2 H)](g_3 H) = (g_1 g_2 H)(g_3 H) = (g_1 g_2 )g_3 H = g_1 (g_2 g_3 )H = (g_1 H)(g_2 g_3 H) = (g_1 H)[(g_2 H)(g_3 H)]$. Hence, G1 is satisfied. If $e$ is the identity element in $G$, then $(g_H)(e_H) = (g_e)H = g_H = (e_g)H = (e_H)(g_H)$ for all $g \in G$. Hence, G2 is satisfied with $e_H = H$ as the identity element in $G/H$. Finally, for each $g \in G$, we have $(g_H)(g^{-1} H) = (g_{g-1} H) = e_H = (g^{-1} g)H = (g^{-1} H)(g_H)$. Hence, G3 is satisfied with $(g_H)^{-1} = g^{-1} H$. Thus $G/H$ is a group.

items.statement: For $f:X\to Y$ and $A\subseteq X$, the restriction $f|_A:A\to Y$ is the same rule as $f$ but with domain reduced to $A$.
items.formal_statement: $f|_A(a)=f(a)$ for every $a\in A$.
items.definition: A function obtained by limiting the domain of another function.
items.intuition: Use the same map on a smaller input set.
items.label: Restriction of a Map
items.gloss: Use the same rule on a smaller domain. Restrictions appear constantly, e.g. $p|_{V_\lambda}$ in covering maps.
items.example: If $f|_R$ is injective, then $f|_R\colon R\to f(R)$ is a bijection.

items.statement: For a subspace $A\subseteq X$, a continuous $r\colon X\to A$ is a \textbf{retraction} if $r(a)=a$ for all $a\in A$. Then $A$ is a \textbf{retract} of $X$.
items.intuition: A continuous way of collapsing $X$ onto $A$ that leaves $A$ fixed. Retractions constrain how $\pi_1(A)$ sits inside $\pi_1(X)$.
items.label: Retraction
items.gloss: A continuous way of collapsing $X$ onto $A$ that leaves $A$ fixed. Retractions constrain how $\pi_1(A)$ sits inside $\pi_1(X)$.
items.example: $S^1$ is a retract of $\mathbb{R}^2\setminus\{0\}$ via $r(x)=x/\lVert x\rVert$.

items.statement: A path-connected space $X$ is \textbf{simply connected} if $\pi_1(X,x_0)$ is trivial for some (hence every) $x_0\in X$.
items.intuition: Path-connected and every loop contracts to a point — no $1$-dimensional holes.
items.label: Simply Connected Space
items.gloss: Path-connected and every loop contracts to a point — no $1$-dimensional holes.
items.example: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is simply connected; $S^1$ and the torus $T^2$ are not.

items.statement: A subset $H\subseteq G$ is a \textbf{subgroup} ($H\le G$) if $*$ restricts to a binary operation on $H$ making $(H,*|_{H\times H})$ a group. It is \textbf{proper} ($H<G$) if $H\subsetneq G$.
items.intuition: A subset that is itself a group under the same operation. The trivial subgroup $\{e\}$ always qualifies.
items.label: Subgroup
items.gloss: A subset that is itself a group under the same operation. The trivial subgroup $\{e\}$ always qualifies.
items.example: $m\mathbb{Z}=\{mn\mid n\in\mathbb{Z}\}$ is a proper subgroup of $\mathbb{Z}$ for $m\ge 2$.

items.statement: $B$ is a subset of $A$, written $B\subseteq A$, if every element of $B$ is an element of $A$. If $B\subseteq A$ and $B\ne A$, then $B$ is a proper subset of $A$.
items.formal_statement: $B\subseteq A\Longleftrightarrow \forall x\,(x\in B\Rightarrow x\in A)$.
items.definition: The inclusion relation between sets.
items.intuition: All points of the smaller set are already inside the larger set.
items.label: Subset
items.gloss: Everything in $B$ is already in $A$. Sets are equal iff each is a subset of the other.
items.example: $\{1,2\}\subsetneq\{1,2,3\}$ and $\mathbb{Z}\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.

items.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is injective if distinct inputs have distinct outputs.
items.formal_statement: $f(x_1)=f(x_2)\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$.
items.definition: A function with no collisions: different inputs map to different outputs.
items.intuition: No two distinct points in the domain get sent to the same point in the codomain.
items.label: Injective
items.gloss: No two distinct points in the domain get sent to the same point in the codomain.
items.example: $f(x)=2x$ is injective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

items.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is surjective if every element of $Y$ is hit.
items.formal_statement: $\forall y\in Y\,\exists x\in X:f(x)=y$.
items.definition: A function with no missed targets: every point in the codomain has a preimage.
items.intuition: Every point in the codomain gets hit by at least one point in the domain.
items.label: Surjective
items.gloss: Every point in the codomain gets hit by at least one point in the domain.
items.example: $f(x)=x$ is surjective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

items.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is bijective if it is both injective and surjective.
items.formal_statement: $f:X\to Y$ is bijective iff for every $y\in Y$ there exists a unique $x\in X$ such that $f(x)=y$.
items.definition: A function that is both injective and surjective: a perfect one-to-one correspondence between the domain and codomain.
items.intuition: Every point in the codomain gets hit by exactly one point in the domain.
items.label: Bijective
items.gloss: A function that is both injective and surjective: a perfect one-to-one correspondence between the domain and codomain.
items.example: $f(x)=x$ is bijective, but $f(x)=x^2$ is not.

items.statement: A function $f:X\to Y$ is injective if distinct inputs have distinct outputs, surjective if every element of $Y$ is hit, and bijective if both hold.
items.formal_statement: Injective: $f(x_1)=f(x_2)\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$. Surjective: $\forall y\in Y\,\exists x\in X:f(x)=y$. Bijective: injective and surjective.
items.definition: The standard one-to-one, onto, and one-to-one-correspondence properties of functions.
items.intuition: Injective means no collisions; surjective means no missed targets; bijective means perfect pairing.
items.label: Surjective / Injective / Bijective
items.gloss: Surjective hits everything, injective never collides, bijective is a perfect one-to-one matching. A bijection has an inverse $f^{-1}$.
items.example: $f$ is bijective iff there is $g$ with $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_A$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_B$ (Thm. A.10).

items.statement: $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology is connected.
items.intuition: The fundamental connected space. The proof uses the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$.
items.label: The Real Numbers Are Connected
items.gloss: The fundamental connected space. The proof uses the least-upper-bound property of $\mathbb{R}$.
items.example: Consequently all intervals are connected, and $\mathbb{R}^n$ is connected.
items.proof:
Assume that $\mathbb{R}$ is disconnected, i.e., that there is a separation of $\mathbb{R}$. Let $U$ and $V$ be a separation of $\mathbb{R}$, and choose $a \in U$ and $b \in V$. We may assume without loss of generality that $a < b$. Let $A = [a, b] \cap U$ and $B = [a, b] \cap V$. Then the pair $A$ and $B$ is a separation of $[a, b]$, with $a \in A$ and $b \in B$. Also note that $A$ is bounded above by $b$. Hence, by the least upper bound property $A$ has a least upper bound; $c =$ sup $A$. Thus $a \leq c \leq b$. We will show that $c$ belongs neither to $A$ nor to $B$ thus contradicting the fact that $A$ and $B$ is a separation of $[a, b]$. Assume that $c \in B$. Since $a 
otin B$ and $B$ is open in $[a, b]$, it follows that there is a real number $d$ such that $a < d < c$ and $(d, c] \subseteq B$. This implies that $d$ is an upper bound of $A$ and that $d$ is less than the least upper bound $c$. That is a contradiction, and so, $c 
otin B$. Now assume that $c \in A$. Since $A$ is open in $[a, b]$ and $b 
otin A$, there is a real number $d$ such that $[c, d) \subseteq A$. For any $e \in (c, d)$ it follows that $e \in A$ and $e > c$. That is a contradiction to the fact that $c$ is an upper bound of $A$. Thus $c 
otin A$. Hence, $c 
otin A$ and $c 
otin B$. This is a contradiction to the fact that $c \in [a, b]$ and that $A$ and $B$ is a separation of $[a, b]$. Thus $\mathbb{R}$ must be connected.

proof_steps.label: Assume a Separation
proof_steps.content: Assume $\mathbb{R}$ is separated by nonempty disjoint open sets $U$ and $V$, and choose $a\in U$, $b\in V$ with $a<b$.

proof_steps.label: Restrict to an Interval
proof_steps.content: The sets $A=[a,b]\cap U$ and $B=[a,b]\cap V$ separate $[a,b]$, with $a\in A$ and $b\in B$.

proof_steps.label: Use the Least Upper Bound
proof_steps.content: Let $c=\sup A$. Openness of $B$ rules out $c\in B$, and openness of $A$ rules out $c\in A$.

proof_steps.label: Contradiction
proof_steps.content: Since $c\in[a,b]$ but $c$ belongs to neither side of the separation, contradiction. Therefore $\mathbb{R}$ is connected.

items.statement: A \textbf{topological property} is any property expressible in terms of points and open sets; such properties are preserved by homeomorphisms. A \textbf{topological invariant} is a quantity invariant under homeomorphism.
items.intuition: These are exactly the features topology can see. If $X\cong Y$ and $X$ has the property, so does $Y$ — hence invariants can prove two spaces are \emph{not} homeomorphic.
items.label: Topological Property / Invariant
items.gloss: These are exactly the features topology can see. If $X\cong Y$ and $X$ has the property, so does $Y$ — hence invariants can prove two spaces are \emph{not} homeomorphic.
items.example: Connectedness, compactness, Hausdorffness and the fundamental group are all topological invariants.

items.statement: For a collection $\mathcal{S}$ of subsets of $X$, the \textbf{topology generated by} $\mathcal{S}$ is $\langle\mathcal{S}\rangle=\bigcap\{\mathcal{T}\ \text{topology}\mid \mathcal{S}\subseteq\mathcal{T}\}$, the smallest topology containing $\mathcal{S}$.
items.intuition: Take any collection of “intended open sets” and form the coarsest topology making them open, by intersecting all topologies that contain them.
items.label: Topology Generated by a Collection
items.gloss: Take any collection of “intended open sets” and form the coarsest topology making them open, by intersecting all topologies that contain them.
items.example: $\langle\varnothing\rangle=\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ind}}$; the collection of all singletons generates $\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{disc}}$.

items.statement: Let $Y$ be compact. If $x\in X$ and $U$ is open in $X\times Y$ containing the slice $\{x\}\times Y$, then there is a neighborhood $W$ of $x$ with $W\times Y\subseteq U$.
items.intuition: An open set containing a whole compact slice automatically contains a “tube” $W\times Y$ around it. The technical engine behind compactness of products.
items.label: Tube Lemma
items.gloss: An open set containing a whole compact slice automatically contains a “tube” $W\times Y$ around it. The technical engine behind compactness of products.
items.example: Used directly to prove that a finite product of compact spaces is compact (Thm. 6.39).
items.proof: As $U$ is open in $X \times Y$ and $(x, y) \in \{x\} \times Y \subseteq U$ for all $y \in Y$, there is a basis element $W_y \times V_y \subseteq U$ for the product topology on $X \times Y$ such that $(x, y) \in W_y \times V_y$. The collection $\{V_y \}y\in Y$ is an open cover of $Y$. Since $Y$ is compact there must be a finite subcover of $\{V_y \}y\in Y$, say, $\{V_{y 1}, V_{y 2}, \ldots, V_{y n} \}$. Let $n \bigcap W = W_{y i}. i=1$ Then $W$ is open in $X$, and it must contain $x$. Clearly, $n ( ) \bigcup W \times Y \subseteq W_{y i} \times V_{y i} \subseteq U. i=1$ Thus $\{x\} \times Y \subseteq W \times Y$ and $W \times Y \subseteq U$. The lemma is not true if we remove the assumption that $Y$ is compact; the open set $U = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} \mid |x_y| < 1\} \subseteq \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}$ does contain {0} $\times \mathbb{R}$ but does not contain any tube $W \times \mathbb{R}$ containing {0} $\times \mathbb{R}.$ (Here we have assumed that $\mathbb{R}$ is given the standard topology, and hence, it is not compact, cf. Example 6.32.)

proof_steps.label: Choose Product Neighborhoods
proof_steps.content: For each $y\in Y$, choose a basic product neighborhood $W_y\times V_y\subseteq U$ containing $(x,y)$.

proof_steps.label: Use Compactness of Y
proof_steps.content: The sets $V_y$ cover $Y$, so compactness gives a finite subcover $V_{y_1},\ldots,V_{y_n}$.

proof_steps.label: Intersect the X-Neighborhoods
proof_steps.content: Let $W=W_{y_1}\cap\cdots\cap W_{y_n}$. Then $W$ is a neighborhood of $x$ and $W\times Y\subseteq U$.

items.statement: Let $p\colon E\to B$ be a covering map with $p(e_0)=b_0$. For any path $f\colon I\to B$ with $f(0)=b_0$ there is a \textbf{unique} path $\tilde f\colon I\to E$ lifting $f$ with $\tilde f(0)=e_0$.
items.intuition: Paths lift uniquely once you fix a starting point upstairs. The cornerstone for relating loops in $B$ to points in a fibre.
items.label: Unique Path Lifting Property
items.gloss: Paths lift uniquely once you fix a starting point upstairs. The cornerstone for relating loops in $B$ to points in a fibre.
items.example: For $p\colon\mathbb{R}\to S^1$, a loop winding once lifts to a path in $\mathbb{R}$ from $0$ to $1$.
items.proof:
For each $s \in I$, let $U_s$ be a neighborhood of $f (s)$ that is evenly covered by $p$. The collection $\mathcal{A} = \{f^{-1} (U_s ) \mid s \in I\}$ is then an open cover of $I$. Since $I$ is a compact metric space, it follows from Lemma 8.10 that there is a Lebesgue number $\lambda > 0$ for $\mathcal{A}$. Consider a subdivision of $I$ consisting of points 0 = $s_0 < s_1 < \cdots < s_n = 1$ such that $s_i - s_{i-1} < 2\lambda$ for 1 $\leq i \leq n$. Then each interval $[s_{i-1}, s_i ]$ lies in one of the preimages $f^{-1} (U_s )$. Thus $f ([s_{i-1}, s_i ]) \subseteq U_s$ for some $s \in I$. We must show that there is a lift $	ilde{f}$ of $f$ such that $	ilde{f}(0) = e_0$ and that this lift is unique. We prove the existence of $	ilde{f}$ first. Assume that $[a, b] \subseteq I$. Let $U$ be a neighborhood of $f (a)$ that is evenly covered by $p$ such that $f ([a, b]) \subseteq U$, and let $ igsqcup p^{-1} (U ) = V_\omega. \omega\in\Omega$ Note that if $e \in p^{-1} (f (a))$ then $e \in V_\omega'$ for a unique $\omega' \in \Omega$. Since $p|V_\omega': V_\omega' 	o U$ is a homeomorphism, it follows that the map ( )$-1 	ilde{g} = p|V_\omega' \circ f |[a,b]: [a, b] 	o E$ is continuous, and moreover, $p\circ	ilde{g} = f |[a,b]$ and $	ilde{g}(a) = e$. From this observation we get that there is a continuous map ( )$-1 	ilde{g}_1 = p|V_\omega \circ f |[s_0,s_1 ]: [s_0, s_1 ] 	o E$ where $p\circ	ilde{g}_1 = f |[s_0,s_1 ]$ and $	ilde{g}_1 (s_0 ) = e_0$. Now let $e_1 = 	ilde{g}_1 (s_1 )$ and proceed as before. That is, $	ilde{g}_2 = ( )^{-1} p|V_\omega \circ f |[s_1,s_2 ]: [s_1, s_2 ] 	o E$ is a continuous map where $p\circ 	ilde{g}_2 = f |[s_1,s_2 ]$ and $	ilde{g}_2 (s_1 ) = e_1$. Continuing this process, we get a continuous map ( )$-1 	ilde{g}_i = p|V_\omega \circ f |[s_{i-1},s_i ]: [s_{i-1}, s_i ] 	o E i^{-1} g_i = f |[s_{i-1},s_i ]$ and and where $p\circ 	ilde{	ilde{g}}_i (s_{i-1} ) = 	ilde{g}_i^{-1} (s_{i-1} ) = e_{i-1}$ for $i \in \{3, 4, \ldots, n\}$. By Lemma 7.3, we can combine $	ilde{g}_1, 	ilde{g}_2, \ldots, 	ilde{g}_n$ into a continuous map $	ilde{f}: I 	o E$ given by $	ilde{f}(s) = 	ilde{g}_i (s)$ for $s \in [s_{i-1}, s_i ]$. Then $	ilde{f}$ is a lift of $f$ such that $	ilde{f}(0) = e_0$. We conclude the proof by showing the uniqueness of $	ilde{f}$. Assume that there are two continuous maps $	ilde{f}_1: I 	o E$ and $	ilde{f}_2: I 	o E$ such that $p\circ	ilde{f}_1 = p\circ	ilde{f}_2 = f$ and $	ilde{f}_1 (0) = 	ilde{f}_2 (0) = e_0$. We want to show that $	ilde{f}_1 = 	ilde{f}_2$. Let $A = \{s \in I \mid 	ilde{f}_1 (s) = 	ilde{f}_2 (s)\}$ and $D = \{s \in I \mid 	ilde{f}_1 (s) 
eq 	ilde{f}_2 (s)\}$. Then clearly, $I = A \cup D$ and $A \cap D = \varnothing$. If we can show that both $A$ are $D$ are open, then because $I$ is connected we have $A = I$ and $D = \varnothing$ as 0 $\in A$, and hence, $	ilde{f}_1 = 	ilde{f}_2$. Let $s \in A$, and let $U$ be a neighborhood of $f (s)$ that is evenly covered by $p$. Assume that $V_\omega$ is the subset of $p^{-1} (U )$ that contains $	ilde{f}_1 (s) = 	ilde{f}_2 (s)$. Let $\mathcal{U} = 	ilde{f}_1-1 (V_\omega ) \cap 	ilde{f}_2-1 (V_\omega )$. Then $\mathcal{U}$ is a neighborhood ( ) ( both) $f_1$ and $f_2$ map all points in $\mathcal{U}$ to $V_\omega$. Thus for all $t \in \mathcal{U}$, we have of $s$ and $p\circ	ilde{f}_1 (t) = f (t) = p\circ	ilde{f}_2 (t)$ implying that $	ilde{f}_1 (t) = 	ilde{f}_2 (t)$ as $p|V: V_\omega 	o U$ is a homeomorphism. $\omega$ Hence, $t \in A$ and $\mathcal{U} \subseteq A$. Thus $A$ is open. Finally, we need to show that $D$ is open. Choose an $s \in D$. Let $U$ be a neighborhood of $f (s)$ that is evenly covered by $p$. Since $	ilde{f}_1 (s) 
eq 	ilde{f}_2 (s)$, there are unique indexes $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ in $\Omega$ such that $	ilde{f}_1 (s) \in V_{\omega 1}$ and $	ilde{f}_2 (s) \in V_{\omega 2}$. Then $\mathcal{U} ' = 	ilde{f}_1-1 (V_{\omega 1} ) \cap 	ilde{f}_2-1 (V_{\omega 2} )$ is a neighborhood of $s$. Since $V_{\omega 1}$ and $V_{\omega 2}$ are disjoint, it follows that $	ilde{f}_1 (t) 
eq 	ilde{f}_2 (t)$ for all $t \in \mathcal{U} '$. Thus $\mathcal{U} ' \subseteq D$. Hence, $D$ is open. Thus $	ilde{f}_1 = 	ilde{f}_2$.

items.statement: The quotient topology is the unique topology on $A$ such that for every space $Y$ and every map $f\colon A\to Y$: $f$ is continuous iff $f\circ\pi\colon X\to Y$ is continuous.
items.intuition: A clean test: a map \emph{out of} a quotient is continuous exactly when its composite with $\pi$ is. This characterises the quotient topology completely.
items.label: Universal Property of the Quotient
items.gloss: A clean test: a map \emph{out of} a quotient is continuous exactly when its composite with $\pi$ is. This characterises the quotient topology completely.
items.example: Lets you define continuous maps on glued spaces by defining them on $X$ in a $\sim$-compatible way.
items.proof: We follow the arguments for the proofs of Theorem 5.6 and Theorem 5.13 and adapt them to our current setting. We first prove that the quotient topology $\mathcal{T} \pi$ has the universal property that for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f: A \to Y, f$ is continuous if and only if $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Let $A$ be given the quotient topology induced by $\pi$, and assume that $f: A \to Y$ is continuous. By definition of the quotient topology $\pi^{-1} (U )$ is open in $X$ if and only if $U$ is open in $A$. Hence, $\pi$ is continuous. Thus by Theorem 3.16 $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Now assume that $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. Let $V$ be an open set in $Y$. Since $\pi$ is a quotient map and $(f \circ\pi)^{-1} (V ) = \pi^{-1} (f^{-1} (V ))$ is open in $X$ by assumption of continuity of $f \circ\pi$, it follows that $f^{-1} (V )$ is open in $A$. Thus $f$ is continuous. Let $\mathcal{T} '$ be a topology on $A$ with the universal property that for every topological space $Y$ and every map $f: A \to Y, f$ is continuous if and only if $f \circ\pi: X \to Y$ is continuous. We must show that $\mathcal{T} ' = \mathcal{T} \pi$. Let $\mathcal{T}$ be the topology on $X$, and let $A$ be given the topology $\mathcal{T} '$. First let $Y = A$ with the quotient topology induced by $f \circ\pi$. Then for $f =$ id $: (A, \mathcal{T} ' ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} \pi )$, we have id $\circ\pi = \pi: (X, \mathcal{T} ) \to (A, \mathcal{T} \pi )$ which is continuous. Hence, by the universal property id is continuous.

proof_steps.label: Quotient Topology Gives the Universal Property
proof_steps.content: If $A$ has the quotient topology induced by $\pi:X\to A$ and $f:A\to Y$ is continuous, then $f\circ\pi$ is continuous by composition.

proof_steps.label: Use the Defining Pullback Test
proof_steps.content: Conversely, if $f\circ\pi$ is continuous and $V\subseteq Y$ is open, then $(f\circ\pi)^{-1}(V)=\pi^{-1}(f^{-1}(V))$ is open in $X$. By the quotient topology, $f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $A$.

proof_steps.label: Uniqueness of the Topology
proof_steps.content: Any topology on $A$ with this universal property has the same open sets as the quotient topology, by applying the property to the identity maps between the two candidate topologies.

items.statement: If $h\colon X\to Y$ is a homeomorphism with $h(x_0)=y_0$, then $h_*\colon\pi_1(X,x_0)\to\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ is an isomorphism.
items.intuition: Homeomorphic spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups — so different $\pi_1$ proves spaces are not homeomorphic.
items.label: π₁ Is a Topological Invariant
items.gloss: Homeomorphic spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups — so different $\pi_1$ proves spaces are not homeomorphic.
items.example: Since $\pi_1(\mathbb{R}^2)=0$ but $\pi_1(S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}$, the plane and the circle are not homeomorphic.
items.proof: We must show that $h*$ is bijective. Let $k: Y \to X$ be the inverse homemorphism of $h$ such that $k(y_0 ) = x_0$. Then, by Theorem 7.24, $k* \circ h* = (k\circ h)* =$ (id$X )*$, and $h* \circ k* = (h\circ k)* =$ (id$Y )*$. Hence, $k* = (h* )^{-1}$.

proof_steps.label: Choose the Inverse Homeomorphism
proof_steps.content: Let $k:Y\to X$ be the inverse homeomorphism of $h:X\to Y$.

proof_steps.label: Apply Functoriality
proof_steps.content: Functoriality gives $k_*\circ h_*=(k\circ h)_*=(\operatorname{id}_X)_*$ and $h_*\circ k_*=(h\circ k)_*=(\operatorname{id}_Y)_*$.

proof_steps.label: Identify the Inverse
proof_steps.content: Thus $k_*$ is the inverse of $h_*$, so $h_*$ is bijective and the fundamental group is a topological invariant.

items.statement: For based spaces $(X,x_0),(Y,y_0)$: $\pi_1(X\times Y,(x_0,y_0))\cong\pi_1(X,x_0)\times\pi_1(Y,y_0)$.
items.intuition: The fundamental group of a product is the direct product of the fundamental groups — loops split into their coordinate loops.
items.label: π₁ of a Product
items.gloss: The fundamental group of a product is the direct product of the fundamental groups — loops split into their coordinate loops.
items.example: $\pi_1(T^2)=\pi_1(S^1\times S^1)\cong\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{Z}$.
items.proof: Let pr 1 $: X \times Y \to X$ and pr 2 $: X \times Y \to Y$ be the projection maps onto the first and second factor, respectively. Define the map $\phi: \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 )) \to \pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \times \pi_1 (Y, y_0 )$ by ( ) ( ) $\phi([h]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) =$ [pr 1 $\circ h],$ [pr 2 $\circ h]$. Note that $\pi_1 (X, x_0 ) \times \pi_1 (Y, y_o )$ is a group with binary operation given by $([f ], [g]) * ([f ' ], [g ' ]) = ([f ] * [f ' ], [g] * [g ' ])$ for $[f ], [f ' ] \in \pi_1 (X, x_0 )$ and $[g], [g ' ] \in \pi_1 (Y, y_0 )$. We will show that $\phi$ is an isomorphism. It is a homomorphism since (pr 1 )$*$ and (pr 2 )$*$ are homomorphisms: ( ) $\phi([h] * [k]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h] * [k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h] * [k]) ( ) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 1 )$* ([k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 2 )$* ([k]) ( ) ( ) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) *$ (pr 1 )$* ([k]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([k]) = \phi([h]) * \phi([k])$ for $[h], [k] \in \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 ))$. Let $f: I \to X$ be a loop in $X$ based at $x_0$, and let $g: I \to Y$ be a loop in $Y$ based at $y_0$. Define the map $h: I \to X \times Y$ by $h(s) = (f (s), g(s))$. Then $h$ is a loop in $X \times Y$ based at $(x_0, y_0 )$, and ( ) ( ) $\phi([h]) =$ (pr 1 )$* ([h]),$ (pr 2 )$* ([h]) =$ [pr 1 $\circ h],$ [pr 2 $\circ h] = ([f ], [g])$. Hence, $\phi$ is surjective. To show that $\phi$ is injective, assume that $\phi([h]) = \phi([k])$ for $[h], [k] \in \pi_1 (X \times Y, (x_0, y_0 ))$. Then there is a path homotopy $H_1: I \times I \to X$ from pr 1 $\circ h$ to pr 1 $\circ k$ and a path homotopy $H_2: I \times I \to Y$ from pr 2 $\circ h$ to pr 2 $\circ k$. Define the map $H: I \times I \to X \times Y$ by ( ) $H(s, t) = H_1 (s, t), H_2 (s, t)$. Then $H$ is a path homotopy from $h =$ (pr 1 $\circ h$, pr 2 $\circ h)$ to $k =$ (pr 1 $\circ k$, pr 2 $\circ k)$. Hence, $[h] = [k]$, and so, $\phi$ is injective. Thus $\phi$ is an isomorphism.

proof_steps.label: Define the Projection-Induced Map
proof_steps.content: Define $\phi:\pi_1(X\times Y,(x_0,y_0))\to\pi_1(X,x_0)\times\pi_1(Y,y_0)$ by applying the two projection-induced homomorphisms.

proof_steps.label: It Is a Homomorphism
proof_steps.content: The map $\phi$ is a homomorphism because each projection-induced map is a homomorphism and the product group operation is coordinatewise.

proof_steps.label: Surjectivity
proof_steps.content: Given loops $f$ in $X$ and $g$ in $Y$, the loop $h(s)=(f(s),g(s))$ in $X\times Y$ maps to $([f],[g])$, so $\phi$ is surjective.

proof_steps.label: Injectivity
proof_steps.content: If two loops have path-homotopic projections in both coordinates, combine those homotopies coordinatewise to get a path homotopy in $X\times Y$. Hence $\phi$ is injective and therefore an isomorphism.

items.statement: For any $s_0\in S^1$, the lifting correspondence $\Phi\colon\pi_1(S^1,s_0)\to\mathbb{Z}$ is a group isomorphism.
items.intuition: The fundamental group of the circle is the integers, with the integer counting how many times a loop winds around. The first non-trivial $\pi_1$ computation.
items.label: π₁(S¹) ≅ ℤ
items.gloss: The fundamental group of the circle is the integers, with the integer counting how many times a loop winds around. The first non-trivial $\pi_1$ computation.
items.example: It underlies the $2$-dimensional Brouwer theorem and the fundamental theorem of algebra.
items.proof: Since $S^{1}$ is path-connected, it follows by Theorem 7.19 that we only need to compute $\pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 )$ for one basepoint (and hence, all) $s_0 \in S^{1}$. By Theorem 8.6, we know that the map $p: \mathbb{R} \to S^{1}$ given by $p(t) =$ (cos(2$\pi t)$, sin(2$\pi t))$ is a covering map. Furthermore, since $\mathbb{R}$ is simply connected, it follows by Theorem 8.15 that the lifting correspondence $\Phi: \pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 ) \to p^{-1} (s_0 )$ is a bijection. Let $e_0 = 0$ such that $p(e_0 ) = p(0) = (1, 0) = s_0$. Clearly, $p^{-1} (s_0 ) = \mathbb{Z}$. We want to show that $\Phi$ is a homomorphism. In other words, if $[f ], [g] \in \pi_1 (S^{1}, s_0 )$ we want to show that $\Phi([f ] * [g]) = \Phi([f ]) + \Phi([g])$. Assume that $f: I \to S^{1}$ and $g: I \to S^{1}$ are two loops in $S^{1}$ based at $s_0$. By Theorem 8.12 there are unique lifts $\tilde{f}$ and $\tilde{g}$ of $f$ and $g$, respectively, such that $\tilde{f}(0) = \tilde{g}(0) = 0$. Let $\tilde{f}(1) = x$ and $\tilde{g}(1) = y$ such that, by definition of $\Phi$, we have $\Phi([f ]) = x$ and $\Phi([g]) = y$. We will show that $\Phi([f * g]) = x + y$. To compute $\Phi([f * g])$ we must lift $f * g$ to a path $\tilde{f} * g$ in $\mathbb{R}$ such that $(\tilde{f} * g)(0) = 0$. Let $\tilde{h}: I \to \mathbb{R}$ be a path in $\mathbb{R}$ given by $\tilde{h}(s) = \tilde{f}(1) + \tilde{g}(s) = x + \tilde{g}(s)$. Note that $\tilde{h}$ is a lift of $g$ starting at $x$. Let $\tilde{f} * \tilde{h}: I \to \mathbb{R}$ be a path in $\mathbb{R}$ given by { $\tilde{f}(2s) 0 \leq s \leq 1/2 (\tilde{f} * \tilde{h})(s) = \tilde{h}(2s^{-1}) 1/2 \leq s \leq 1$.

items.label: Arbitrary Union of Open Sets Is Open
items.statement: In a topological space, any union of open sets is open.
items.formal_statement: $$\{U_i\}_{i\in I}\subseteq\tau_X\Longrightarrow\bigcup_{i\in I}U_i\in\tau_X.$$
items.intuition: Open sets may be glued together by arbitrary unions without leaving the topology.
items.canonical_label: arbitrary union of open sets is open
items.display_label: Arbitrary Union of Open Sets Is Open

items.statement: An open neighborhood of x is an open set containing x.
items.formal_statement: $$U\text{ is an open neighborhood of }x\Longleftrightarrow U\in\mathcal T\ \text{and}\ x\in U.$$
items.definition: In a topological space $(X,\mathcal T)$, an open neighborhood of $x\in X$ is an open set $U\in\mathcal T$ with $x\in U$.
items.intuition: An open neighborhood is the clean case: the set itself is one of the topology's open sets and it contains the point.
items.label: Open Neighborhood
items.gloss: The stricter neighborhood convention used in some notes: the surrounding set is itself open.
items.example: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, $(-1,1)$ is a neighborhood of $0$ because $0\in(-1,1)$ and $(-1,1)$ is open.
convention_notes: Some authors call this simply a neighborhood.
In this graph, open_neighborhood specializes neighborhood.

items.label: Membership Relation
items.statement: $x\in A$ means that $x$ is an element/member of the set $A$.
items.formal_statement: $\in$ is the primitive relation between objects and sets used to express elementhood.
items.definition: The relation expressing that an object belongs to a set.
items.intuition: The atomic “is in” relation from which subset, union, intersection, and most set constructions are phrased.

items.label: Empty Set
items.statement: The empty set is the unique set with no elements.
items.formal_statement: $\varnothing=\{x\mid x\ne x\}$; equivalently, $\forall x\,(x\notin\varnothing)$.
items.definition: The unique set containing no elements.
items.intuition: The smallest set and the mandatory open set in every topology.

items.label: Power Set
items.statement: The power set of $X$ is the set of all subsets of $X$.
items.formal_statement: $\mathcal P(X)=\{A\mid A\subseteq X\}$.
items.definition: A construction sending a set to the set of all of its subsets.
items.intuition: A topology on $X$ is not just any object; it is a selected subcollection of $\mathcal P(X)$.

items.label: Ordered Pair
items.statement: An ordered pair $(a,b)$ records two objects in order; $(a,b)=(c,d)$ exactly when $a=c$ and $b=d$.
items.formal_statement: $(a,b)=(c,d)\Longleftrightarrow a=c\land b=d$.
items.definition: A two-entry ordered object used to define products and relations.
items.intuition: Unlike sets, order matters: $(a,b)$ and $(b,a)$ need not be the same.

items.label: Relation
items.statement: A relation from $A$ to $B$ is a subset of $A\times B$; a relation on $A$ is a subset of $A\times A$.
items.formal_statement: $R\subseteq A\times B$; write $aRb$ when $(a,b)\in R$.
items.definition: A set of ordered pairs.
items.intuition: A relation records which pairs of elements are linked. Functions, orders, and equivalence relations are special relations.

items.label: Equivalence Relation
items.statement: An equivalence relation on $X$ is a relation $\sim$ that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
items.formal_statement: $\forall x\,x\sim x$; $x\sim y\Rightarrow y\sim x$; $(x\sim y\land y\sim z)\Rightarrow x\sim z$.
items.definition: A relation that partitions a set into equivalence classes.
items.intuition: It formalizes “same for the purpose at hand” and is the set-theoretic input for quotient constructions.

items.label: Domain
items.statement: The domain of a function is the set of inputs on which the function is defined.
items.formal_statement: For $f:X\to Y$, $\operatorname{dom}(f)=X$.
items.definition: The input set of a function.
items.intuition: The side where function evaluation starts.

items.label: Codomain
items.statement: The codomain of a function is the declared target set of its outputs.
items.formal_statement: For $f:X\to Y$, $\operatorname{cod}(f)=Y$.
items.definition: The target set specified as part of a function.
items.intuition: The set a function is allowed to land in; the image may be smaller.

items.label: Preimage / Inverse Image
items.statement: For a function $f:X\to Y$ and a subset $B\subseteq Y$, the preimage (or inverse image) of $B$ is the set of all inputs whose values land in $B$.
items.definition: The subset of the domain whose values lie in a given subset of the codomain.
items.intuition: Preimages pull questions about subsets of the codomain back to subsets of the domain. This is why continuity is naturally stated using preimages of open sets.
items.gloss: Also called the inverse image of $B$ under $f$; this is a set, not the inverse relation $f^{-1}\subseteq Y\times X$.
items.example: For $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ with $f(x)=x^2$, the preimage of $[0,1]$ is $[-1,1]$.

items.label: Identity Map
items.statement: The identity map on $X$ is the function $\operatorname{id}_X:X\to X$ given by $\operatorname{id}_X(x)=x$.
items.formal_statement: $\operatorname{id}_X(x)=x$ for every $x\in X$.
items.definition: The function that leaves every element fixed.
items.intuition: The neutral element for composition of maps.

items.label: Indexed Family
items.statement: An indexed family $(A_i)_{i\in I}$ is a function assigning to each index $i\in I$ an object $A_i$.
items.formal_statement: An indexed family over $I$ is a function $A:I\to\mathcal U$, usually written $i\mapsto A_i$.
items.definition: A collection whose members are labeled by an index set.
items.intuition: Used for arbitrary unions, open covers, products, and choice functions.

items.label: Choice Function
items.statement: For a family of nonempty sets $(A_i)_{i\in I}$, a choice function is a function $c:I\to\bigcup_{i\in I}A_i$ with $c(i)\in A_i$ for every $i$.
items.formal_statement: $\forall i\in I,\ A_i\ne\varnothing$ and $c(i)\in A_i$.
items.definition: A function selecting one element from each set in a family.
items.intuition: The concrete object whose existence is asserted by the Axiom of Choice.

items.label: Finite Set
items.statement: A set is finite if it has exactly $n$ elements for some natural number $n$.
items.formal_statement: $A$ is finite if $A\cong\{1,\dots,n\}$ for some $n\in\mathbb N$, with $n=0$ corresponding to $\varnothing$.
items.definition: A set bijective with an initial segment of the natural numbers.
items.intuition: The set-theoretic notion behind finite subcovers and finite intersections.

items.label: Finite Subcover
items.statement: A finite subcover is a finite subcollection of a cover that still covers the same space or set.
items.formal_statement: If $(U_i)_{i\in I}$ covers $X$, a finite subcover is $U_{i_1},\dots,U_{i_n}$ with $X\subseteq\bigcup_{k=1}^n U_{i_k}$.
items.definition: A finite subfamily of a cover whose union still contains the covered object.
items.intuition: The key finite extraction demanded by compactness.

conventions.neighborhood: broad_neighborhood; open_neighborhood is the strict open version
conventions.locally_compact_space: compact-neighborhood convention; add Hausdorff assumptions for compact-closure equivalences
conventions.manifold: Hausdorff, second-countable, topological manifold without boundary unless otherwise specified
conventions.compactness: open-cover compactness; not closed-and-bounded except in Euclidean Heine-Borel contexts

labeling.policy: Human-facing item labels use polished Title Case. Stable machine identity remains in id. Previous labels are preserved in canonical_label.

concept_map.description: Concept-level map of spaces, definitions, examples, and motivating relations.
concept_map.example_traversal: topological_space -> defined_by -> topology; compactness -> has_example -> cantor_set; quotient_space_examples -> motivates -> quotient_topology

definition_dependency_graph.example_traversal: tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set

theorem_dependency_graph.description: Logical dependencies among results and assumptions, separated from definitional needs.

proof_graph.description: Proofs, proof steps, proof methods, and results used by particular proofs.

traversal_primitives.outgoing: Follow edges whose source is the current node.
traversal_primitives.incoming: Follow edges whose target is the current node.

example_queries.example: From tychonoff_theorem return product_space, product_topology, compactness, open_set, open_cover_notation, alexander_subbase_theorem, zorns_lemma, and axiom_of_choice grouped by dependency_class.
From urysohn_metrization_theorem follow requires to regularity, hausdorff_property, second_countability, and metric_topology.
From heine_borel_theorem keep targets of kind proof_method: open_cover_argument, finite_subcover_argument.
From tychonoff_theorem follow assumes to axiom_of_choice; from urysohn_metrization_theorem follow assumes to regularity_assumption, hausdorff_assumption, and second_countability_assumption.
From compactness follow has_example to cantor_set and circle; also inspect incoming has_property from examples for additional satisfying instances.
From hausdorff_assumption use incoming shows_necessity_of to get non_hausdorff_spaces_as_counterexamples, then inspect compact_subsets_of_hausdorff_are_closed -> has_counterexample.
From topological_space follow incoming subtype_of; compact_hausdorff_space appears under both compact_space and hausdorff_space.

One path is set -> topology -> open_set -> compactness -> alexander_subbase_theorem -> tychonoff_theorem via prerequisite_for; enrich with tychonoff_theorem -> requires -> product_topology and tychonoff_theorem -> assumes -> axiom_of_choice.
